evidence_tiers.A: Multiple meta-analyses, or meta-analysis plus Mendelian randomization
evidence_tiers.B: Single meta-analysis, or a strong large prospective cohort
evidence_tiers.C: Limited evidence: small, purely observational, or conflicting

profile.mechanism: ApoB is the structural protein of all atherogenic lipoproteins (LDL, VLDL, IDL, Lp(a)); each particle carries exactly one apoB molecule, so apoB concentration equals the number of atherogenic particles. Particle number drives arterial wall infiltration and atherosclerosis, making apoB a more direct causal proxy for atherogenic burden than cholesterol mass.

measurement.method: Immunoturbidimetric or immunonephelometric assay on a standard chemistry analyzer; non-fasting acceptable; standardized internationally.

profile.signal_quality_note: Low biological and analytic variability; non-fasting measurement valid; well-standardized assays. More reproducible than calculated LDL-C.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual; more frequent during lipid-lowering titration

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher apoB, all-cause mortality, fully adjusted
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
all_cause_mortality.study: UK Biobank prospective cohort, apoB and mortality (dose-response analysis).
all_cause_mortality.citation: Dose-Response Associations of Lipid Traits With Coronary Artery Disease and Mortality (UK Biobank, ~347,797 participants), PMC10799266.
all_cause_mortality.notes: ApoB association with all-cause mortality is real but modest (~11% per SD) and substantially weaker than with CAD (~65% per SD) or CVD mortality (~36% per SD). The signal is partly attenuated by reverse causation (low apoB in frailty/illness). Per-SD estimate confirmed from UK Biobank dose-response analysis; CI is approximate.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD (29 mg/dL) higher apoB, coronary heart disease, fully adjusted and mutually lipid-adjusted
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration, individual-participant meta-analysis of 68 prospective studies.
cvd.citation: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration (Di Angelantonio E et al.). Major lipids, apolipoproteins, and risk of vascular disease. JAMA 2009;302:1993-2000. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.1619
cvd.notes: ERFC reported per-SD HRs (1 SD apoB = 29 mg/dL); apoB and non-HDL-C were near-indistinguishable as the strongest lipid predictors of CHD. Causal: Mendelian randomization (UK Biobank, ~348,000 participants) confirms genetically predicted apoB is causally associated with CAD in a dose-dependent manner. ApoB is widely considered the single best causal lipid marker for ASCVD.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: highest vs lowest decile of genetically predicted apoB, cancer-related mortality, Mendelian randomization
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: decile contrast; k = 1/3.51 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cancer.study: UK Biobank one-sample Mendelian randomization of serum apolipoproteins and cause-specific mortality.
cancer.citation: Serum apolipoproteins and mortality risk: evidence from observational and Mendelian randomization analyses. Am J Clin Nutr 2024;119:auth. doi:10.1016/j.ajcnut.2024.01.002
cancer.notes: Genetically predicted apoB (top vs bottom decile) was associated with higher cancer-related mortality (HR 1.39, P-trend <0.0001). The decile contrast was converted to per-SD; the observational per-SD signal for cancer is weaker. Evidence is limited (single MR study); biological pathway for apoB-cancer causality is uncertain. Treat as exploratory.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: highest vs lowest quarter of apoB, incident all-cause dementia, UK Biobank
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
dementia.study: UK Biobank serum lipid traits and dementia cohort (254,575 women, 214,891 men).
dementia.citation: Iwagami M et al. Serum lipid traits and the risk of dementia: a cohort study of the UK Biobank. eClinicalMedicine 2022;53:101695. doi:10.1016/j.eclinm.2022.101695
dementia.notes: UK Biobank found apoB in the highest vs lowest quarter associated with modestly higher all-cause dementia (HR 1.12); the apoB/apoA1 ratio was more clearly graded (HR ~1.23 per SD). A separate MR (Comms Biol 2024) suggested genetically predicted apoB may modestly raise Alzheimer's risk. Quartile contrast converted to per-SD; observational, modest, and partly confounded - treat as indicative.

modifiability.intervention: Statins, ezetimibe, and PCSK9 inhibitors lower apoB; PCSK9 inhibitors and high-intensity statins produce the largest reductions.
modifiability.effect_size: High-intensity statin lowers apoB ~35-45%; adding ezetimibe +15-20%; PCSK9 inhibitors lower apoB 45-55%.
modifiability.citation: CTT Collaboration statin meta-analyses; FOURIER (Sabatine 2017) and ODYSSEY OUTCOMES (Schwartz 2018) PCSK9 RCTs.

profile.mechanism: LDL-C measures the cholesterol mass carried by LDL particles, the dominant atherogenic lipoprotein. Cumulative lifetime LDL-C exposure causally drives atherosclerotic plaque formation; genetic, epidemiologic and trial evidence is concordant.

measurement.method: Calculated (Friedewald/Martin-Hopkins equation from total cholesterol, HDL-C, triglycerides) or direct enzymatic assay; fasting traditionally preferred for calculated values.

profile.signal_quality_note: Calculated LDL-C is biased at high triglycerides or low LDL-C; cholesterol content per particle varies, causing discordance with particle number. Direct assays improve reliability.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual; more frequent during treatment titration

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: U-shaped/non-linear association; no single HR adequately summarizes
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: non-linear U-shaped association; no usable linear per-SD summary
all_cause_mortality.study: UK Biobank and other large cohorts show U-/L-shaped relation of LDL-C with all-cause mortality.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Cholesterol Insights and Controversies from the UK Biobank Study, Circulation 2019; doi:10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.119.042134
all_cause_mortality.notes: Relationship with all-cause mortality is U-shaped: both very low and very high LDL-C associate with higher mortality, with the low-LDL-C signal heavily confounded by reverse causation (illness, frailty, malnutrition; e.g., HR ~5.8 for TC <5.4 mmol/L in untreated elderly cohorts). A single linear per-SD HR is not meaningful; flagged unconvertible. Causal MR evidence for CVD remains strong despite the U-shaped mortality curve.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD (33 mg/dL) higher directly measured LDL-C, coronary heart disease, fully adjusted
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration, individual-participant meta-analysis (subset with directly measured LDL-C).
cvd.citation: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration (Di Angelantonio E et al.). Major lipids, apolipoproteins, and risk of vascular disease. JAMA 2009;302:1993-2000. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.1619
cvd.notes: ERFC per-SD HR for directly measured LDL-C and CHD (1 SD = 33 mg/dL). Causal: extensive MR and RCT evidence (CTT statin meta-analyses, IMPROVE-IT, FOURIER) establishes LDL-C as causal for ASCVD with log-linear benefit of lowering. The short-term observational per-SD HR understates the causal effect of lifetime exposure; MR shows a roughly 3-fold larger effect per mmol/L for lifelong vs short-term exposure.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: no causal association; observational low-LDL-C / cancer link attributed to reverse causation
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: no usable causal/linear cancer HR; observational signal confounded by reverse causation
cancer.study: Mendelian randomization of LDL-C and cancer (combined Copenhagen cohorts) plus comprehensive multi-cancer MR.
cancer.citation: Benn M et al. Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol and the risk of cancer: a Mendelian randomization study. J Natl Cancer Inst 2011;103:508-519. doi:10.1093/jnci/djr008
cancer.notes: Observationally, LOW LDL-C associates with higher cancer risk, but MR shows genetically lowered LDL-C does NOT cause cancer (OR 0.96, 0.87-1.05 per 50% reduction) - the observational signal is reverse causation (preclinical cancer lowers cholesterol). No defensible directional per-SD cancer HR; cell flagged unconvertible/no usable evidence.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: U-shaped association of LDL-C with incident dementia in UK Biobank (non-linear by quarters)
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: non-linear U-shaped association; no usable linear per-SD summary
dementia.study: UK Biobank serum lipid traits and dementia cohort (254,575 women and 214,891 men).
dementia.citation: Iwagami M et al. Serum lipid traits and the risk of dementia: a cohort study of the UK Biobank. eClinicalMedicine 2022;53:101695. doi:10.1016/j.eclinm.2022.101695
dementia.notes: LDL-C showed a non-linear (U-shaped by quarters) relation to incident dementia; the apolipoprotein ratios were the more clearly graded predictors. No defensible linear per-SD HR; flagged unconvertible. Mid-life vs late-life LDL-C may act in opposite directions, complicating interpretation.

modifiability.intervention: Statins (first-line), ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, bempedoic acid; diet (reduced saturated fat, fiber, plant sterols).
modifiability.effect_size: High-intensity statin lowers LDL-C ~50%; ezetimibe ~15-20% additional; PCSK9 inhibitor ~50-60%; diet alone ~5-15%.
modifiability.citation: Cholesterol Treatment Trialists' (CTT) Collaboration meta-analyses, Lancet.

profile.mechanism: LDL-P counts the actual number of LDL particles, the unit that physically infiltrates the arterial wall. When LDL-P and LDL-C are discordant (e.g., insulin resistance, high triglycerides), LDL-P better tracks atherosclerotic risk because particle number, not cholesterol mass, drives plaque.

measurement.method: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) lipoprotein spectroscopy; specialty lab assay.

profile.signal_quality_note: NMR is reproducible but less standardized across platforms than apoB; biologically near-equivalent to apoB (both index particle number).
profile.testing_cadence: baseline; repeat if discordance with LDL-C suspected

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher LDL-P, incident CVD, overall MESA cohort
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA), prospective cohort, NMR-measured LDL-P.
cvd.citation: Otvos JD et al. Clinical implications of discordance between LDL-C and LDL particle number. J Clin Lipidol 2011;5:105-113. PMID:21392724
cvd.notes: In MESA, when LDL-P and LDL-C were discordant, only LDL-P remained associated with incident CVD (HR 1.45, 95% CI 1.19-1.78) while discordant LDL-C was not (HR 1.07, 0.88-1.30). Framingham Offspring similarly found LDL-P ~twice as strongly related to CVD as LDL-C. Observational; LDL-P is biologically near-equivalent to apoB.

modifiability.intervention: Same lipid-lowering therapies as LDL-C (statins, ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors); addressing insulin resistance reduces discordance.
modifiability.effect_size: Statins lower LDL-P broadly in parallel with LDL-C/apoB (~30-50%); residual discordant LDL-P may persist with insulin resistance.
modifiability.citation: Statin trials with NMR substudies; CTT-class evidence extrapolated via apoB equivalence.

profile.mechanism: Small, dense LDL particles penetrate the arterial wall more readily, are retained longer, and are more susceptible to oxidation than large buoyant LDL. A predominance of small dense LDL (pattern B) marks an atherogenic dyslipidemia typical of insulin resistance.

measurement.method: NMR spectroscopy, gradient gel electrophoresis, or ion mobility; small-dense LDL-cholesterol can also be measured by a direct homogeneous assay.

profile.signal_quality_note: Size is strongly correlated with triglycerides and particle number; once particle number (apoB/LDL-P) is accounted for, independent predictive value of size is weak and inconsistent across studies.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline; limited incremental value over apoB/LDL-P

cvd.hr_unit_detail: highest vs lowest quartile of small-dense LDL-cholesterol, incident CHD
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cvd.study: Atherosclerosis Risk in Communities (ARIC) study, prospective cohort, small-dense LDL-C.
cvd.citation: Hoogeveen RC et al. Small dense LDL-cholesterol concentrations predict risk for coronary heart disease: ARIC. Arterioscler Thromb Vasc Biol 2014;34:1069-1077. doi:10.1161/ATVBAHA.114.303284
cvd.notes: A meta-analysis pooled an HR of 2.83 (1.57-5.09) for sdLDL-C and CHD. However, much of the risk attributed to small/dense LDL reflects elevated particle number and triglyceride-rich dyslipidemia; size adds little independent prediction once apoB or LDL-P is known. Observational only. Converted from quartile contrast; sdLDL-C distribution is skewed so the normal-approx conversion is itself an approximation.

modifiability.intervention: Triglyceride-lowering measures: weight loss, carbohydrate reduction, exercise, glycemic control; fibrates and high-dose omega-3 shift LDL toward larger particles.
modifiability.effect_size: Lifestyle and triglyceride reduction can shift LDL phenotype from pattern B to A; magnitude depends on baseline metabolic state.
modifiability.citation: Intervention studies of low-carbohydrate diets and fibrates on LDL subfractions.

biomarkers.name: HDL-C

profile.mechanism: HDL-C measures cholesterol carried by HDL particles, historically viewed as protective via reverse cholesterol transport. However, Mendelian randomization shows HDL-C is not causally protective; it is largely a marker of metabolic health, and both low and very high HDL-C associate with excess mortality.

measurement.method: Direct homogeneous enzymatic assay on standard chemistry analyzer; non-fasting acceptable.

profile.signal_quality_note: Analytically reliable, but biologically a poor causal target; HDL-C reflects metabolic, inflammatory and lifestyle status rather than a modifiable causal pathway.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + periodic with standard lipid panel

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: extreme high HDL-C >=3.5 mmol/L (~135 mg/dL) vs reference, all-cause mortality, women (U-shaped; low HDL-C also elevated risk)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
all_cause_mortality.study: Copenhagen City Heart Study + Copenhagen General Population Study, two prospective cohorts.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Madsen CM et al. Extreme high HDL cholesterol is paradoxically associated with high mortality. Eur Heart J 2017;38:2478-2486. PMID:28419274
all_cause_mortality.notes: Relationship is U-shaped: lowest mortality at HDL-C ~1.9 mmol/L (men) / 2.4 mmol/L (women); both low and extreme-high HDL-C raise all-cause mortality. The cited HR is for extreme-high HDL-C in women vs a mid-range reference, an asymmetric tail contrast that cannot be put on a per-SD scale; flagged unconvertible: HDL-C is a continuous marker with a genuinely U-shaped mortality relation (confirmed in CANHEART, Copenhagen, and Korean/Chinese mega-cohorts), so no single linear per-SD HR is defensible.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD (15 mg/dL) higher HDL-C, coronary heart disease, fully adjusted (observational, not causal)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration, individual-participant meta-analysis of 68 prospective studies.
cvd.citation: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration (Di Angelantonio E et al.). Major lipids, apolipoproteins, and risk of vascular disease. JAMA 2009;302:1993-2000. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.1619
cvd.notes: ERFC per-SD HR (1 SD = 15 mg/dL). Inverse observational association is robust but NOT causal: Mendelian randomization (Voight 2012; CETP-variant studies) and HDL-raising RCTs (niacin, CETP inhibitors) show raising HDL-C does not reduce CVD events. HDL-C should be read as a risk marker, not a treatment target.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: extreme high HDL-C 96th-99th percentile vs 41st-60th percentile, any dementia, men (U-shaped; low HDL-C also elevated risk)
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
dementia.study: Copenhagen City Heart Study + Copenhagen General Population Study, prospective cohorts.
dementia.citation: Madsen CM et al. Plasma high-density lipoprotein cholesterol and risk of dementia: observational and genetic studies. Eur Heart J 2021. PMID:33964140
dementia.notes: Both high and low HDL-C associate with higher dementia risk (U-shaped); extreme-high HDL-C HR ~1.66-2.00 for any dementia. A Kaiser Permanente cohort (184,367) found ~15% higher dementia rate at highest vs middle HDL-C and ~7% at lowest. Genetic (MR) analyses do not support HDL-C as causal; the dementia association is likely confounding. Asymmetric tail contrast, flagged unconvertible.

modifiability.intervention: Exercise, smoking cessation, weight loss, moderate alcohol modestly raise HDL-C; however raising HDL-C has not been shown to reduce events.
modifiability.effect_size: Lifestyle changes raise HDL-C ~5-10%; CETP inhibitors raise it >50% but without clinical benefit.
modifiability.citation: AIM-HIGH, HPS2-THRIVE (niacin); dal-OUTCOMES, ACCELERATE (CETP inhibitors) - all neutral for events.

biomarkers.comparability_note: HDL-C has a U-shaped association with mortality and dementia — both low and very high levels raise risk — so no single per-SD slope represents it; the off-scale cells use an extreme-vs-reference contrast.

profile.mechanism: Cholesterol efflux capacity is a functional assay measuring HDL's ability to accept cholesterol from macrophages, the first step of reverse cholesterol transport. It captures HDL function rather than HDL-C mass, and is inversely associated with cardiovascular events independent of HDL-C level.

measurement.method: Ex vivo assay: serum/apoB-depleted plasma incubated with cholesterol-labeled macrophages; efflux quantified by radiolabel or fluorescence. Research-grade, not standardized for routine clinical use.
measurement.approx_cost_usd: research assay; not routinely priced (estimate 150-400 if offered)

profile.signal_quality_note: No standardized clinical assay; results vary by cell type, acceptor preparation and lab protocol; primarily a research tool.
profile.testing_cadence: research use; not part of routine clinical monitoring

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher cholesterol efflux capacity, incident ASCVD, fully adjusted
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Dallas Heart Study, prospective cohort, cholesterol efflux capacity and incident ASCVD.
cvd.citation: Rohatgi A et al. HDL cholesterol efflux capacity and incident cardiovascular events. N Engl J Med 2014;371:2383-2393. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1409065
cvd.notes: Highest vs lowest quartile HR 0.33 (0.19-0.55). EPIC-Norfolk replicated an inverse association (per-SD OR ~0.80, 0.70-0.90 for incident CHD). A meta-analysis found ~5% lower event risk per 0.1-unit CEC increase. Observational; not causal, and association attenuated/inconsistent in some cohorts (e.g., MESA). No standardized assay.

modifiability.intervention: Exercise, weight loss, and statins modestly improve efflux capacity; no proven therapy reliably raises it with demonstrated clinical benefit.
modifiability.effect_size: Lifestyle and statin effects on CEC are small and variable.
modifiability.citation: Intervention substudies of exercise and statins on HDL function.

profile.mechanism: Triglycerides index triglyceride-rich lipoproteins (VLDL and remnants). The cholesterol carried in these remnant particles is atherogenic; Mendelian randomization links triglyceride-rich lipoprotein pathways causally to coronary disease, though much of TG's risk is mediated through remnant cholesterol and apoB.

measurement.method: Enzymatic colorimetric assay; fasting traditionally preferred, non-fasting increasingly accepted with adjusted thresholds.

profile.signal_quality_note: High within-person biological variability (diet, alcohol, fasting state); single measurements are unstable and skewed.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual; repeat for confirmation given variability

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher triglycerides, all-cause mortality, fully adjusted
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
all_cause_mortality.study: UK Biobank prospective cohort, dose-response analysis of lipid traits and mortality.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Dose-Response Associations of Lipid Traits With Coronary Artery Disease and Mortality (UK Biobank, ~347,797 participants), PMC10799266.
all_cause_mortality.notes: UK Biobank dose-response: per-SD HR 1.08 (all-cause) and 1.21 (CVD mortality). Consistent with the 61-study triglyceride meta-analysis (Liu 2013) which found a per-1-mmol/L all-cause-mortality RR of 1.12 (1.07-1.16) across 38 studies / 330,566 participants. Association modest and substantially attenuated by HDL-C/non-HDL-C adjustment; the per-SD figure is the comparable estimate.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher log-e triglycerides, coronary heart disease, adjusted for non-lipid risk factors
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
cvd.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration, individual-participant meta-analysis of 68 prospective studies.
cvd.citation: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration (Di Angelantonio E et al.). Major lipids, apolipoproteins, and risk of vascular disease. JAMA 2009;302:1993-2000. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.1619
cvd.notes: Reported per 1 SD of log-e triglycerides, adjusted for non-lipid factors. Crucially, this association attenuates to HR 0.99 (0.94-1.05) after further adjustment for HDL-C and non-HDL-C, because TG risk is largely mediated by remnant cholesterol and apoB. MR (Triglyceride Coronary Disease Genetics Consortium) nonetheless supports a causal role for triglyceride-rich lipoproteins. hr_per_sd is 'per SD of log marker', the accepted comparable quantity for this skewed marker.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher triglycerides, incident all-cause dementia, UK Biobank
dementia.study: UK Biobank serum lipid traits and dementia cohort.
dementia.citation: Iwagami M et al. Serum lipid traits and the risk of dementia: a cohort study of the UK Biobank. eClinicalMedicine 2022;53:101695. doi:10.1016/j.eclinm.2022.101695
dementia.notes: UK Biobank found triglycerides slightly INVERSELY associated with incident dementia (HR ~0.96 per SD, CI touching 1.0); ASPREE similarly found higher triglycerides associated with lower dementia risk in older adults (HR 0.82 per doubling). The association is small, age-dependent (possibly opposite in mid-life), and likely reflects reverse causation/competing risk - not a robust protective effect. Treat as exploratory.

modifiability.intervention: Weight loss, carbohydrate/alcohol reduction, exercise, glycemic control; fibrates, high-dose omega-3 fatty acids (icosapent ethyl); statins lower TG modestly.
modifiability.effect_size: Lifestyle can lower TG 20-50%; fibrates ~30-50%; icosapent ethyl ~20%.
modifiability.citation: REDUCE-IT (icosapent ethyl, Bhatt 2019); fibrate trial meta-analyses.

profile.mechanism: The TG/HDL-C ratio is a surrogate for insulin resistance and atherogenic dyslipidemia (high remnants, small dense LDL, low HDL). It captures the metabolic syndrome lipid pattern in a single number and predicts cardiovascular events and incident diabetes.

measurement.method: Calculated from standard lipid panel (triglycerides divided by HDL-C, both in mg/dL or both in mmol/L - units must match).

profile.signal_quality_note: Inherits high variability of triglycerides; ratio is unit-dependent and population-dependent (thresholds differ by ethnicity and mg/dL vs mmol/L).
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + periodic with lipid panel

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher TG/HDL-C ratio, all-cause mortality, general population, fully adjusted
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
all_cause_mortality.study: UK Biobank general-population cohort, TG/HDL-C ratio and all-cause/cause-specific mortality.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Associations of modified triglyceride-glucose indices and the triglyceride/HDL ratio with all-cause and cause-specific mortality in the general population: UK Biobank. Lipids Health Dis 2025;24:124. PMC11963390.
all_cause_mortality.notes: Replaces the earlier non-standardizable categorical cell (~2-fold in high-risk strata) with a native general-population per-SD HR. The effect is small (1.5% per SD) and J-shaped (nadir ratio ~1.08); the earlier ~2-fold figure came from selected diabetic/CAD cohorts and overstated the general-population effect. TG/HDL-C is an insulin-resistance surrogate adding little beyond apoB/non-HDL-C.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher log TG/HDL-C ratio, incident CVD, UK Biobank, fully adjusted
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
cvd.study: UK Biobank general-population cohort, TG/HDL-C ratio and incident cardiovascular disease.
cvd.citation: Che B et al. Triglyceride-glucose index and triglyceride to HDL-C ratio as potential cardiovascular disease risk factors: an analysis of UK Biobank data. Cardiovasc Diabetol 2023;22:34. doi:10.1186/s12933-023-01762-2
cvd.notes: Replaces the earlier non-linear/categorical cell with a native per-log-SD HR 1.12 (Q4-vs-Q1 HR 1.29). The ratio is skewed so the comparable quantity is 'per SD of log ratio'. Dyslipidemia, type 2 diabetes and hypertension account for ~47% of the association; TG/HDL-C is best read as an insulin-resistance surrogate, adding little independent prediction beyond apoB/non-HDL-C.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher apoB/apoA1 ratio (closest large-cohort proxy for atherogenic-dyslipidemia ratio), incident dementia, UK Biobank
dementia.study: UK Biobank serum lipid traits and dementia cohort.
dementia.citation: Iwagami M et al. Serum lipid traits and the risk of dementia: a cohort study of the UK Biobank. eClinicalMedicine 2022;53:101695. doi:10.1016/j.eclinm.2022.101695
dementia.notes: A direct per-SD HR for the TG/HDL-C ratio and dementia was not located. The UK Biobank found atherogenic apolipoprotein ratios (apoB/apoA1, here used as the closest proxy) modestly associated with dementia (~1.12 per SD), while triglycerides alone showed HR ~0.96 per SD. The proxy is imperfect; treat as indicative only. Genetic/MR evidence does not strongly support a causal lipid-ratio effect on dementia.

modifiability.intervention: Weight loss, low-carbohydrate diet, exercise, glycemic control improve the ratio mainly by lowering triglycerides and raising HDL-C.
modifiability.effect_size: Lifestyle interventions can lower the ratio substantially (often 25-50%) by improving insulin sensitivity.
modifiability.citation: Low-carbohydrate diet and exercise intervention studies on TG/HDL-C.

profile.mechanism: Lp(a) is an LDL-like particle with apolipoprotein(a) attached to apoB; it is pro-atherogenic, pro-thrombotic and pro-inflammatory. Levels are ~80-90% genetically determined by the LPA gene. Genetic and epidemiologic evidence establishes Lp(a) as a causal, independent risk factor for ASCVD and aortic stenosis.

measurement.method: Immunoassay; ideally reported in nmol/L (molar) with an isoform-insensitive assay; non-fasting acceptable; usually a one-time test given genetic stability.

profile.signal_quality_note: Highly genetically stable over a lifetime; main analytic concern is isoform-dependent mass assays - molar (nmol/L) assays preferred. Once measured, rarely needs repeating.
confounders: assay isoform sensitivity (mass vs molar units)
profile.testing_cadence: once in a lifetime for most adults (genetically stable); recheck only if new therapy or clinical change

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: no significant association of Lp(a) with non-vascular / all-cause mortality in the Erqou meta-analysis
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: no significant or quantified all-cause mortality association available
all_cause_mortality.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration / Erqou meta-analysis of 36 prospective studies.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Erqou S et al. Lipoprotein(a) concentration and the risk of coronary heart disease, stroke, and nonvascular mortality. JAMA 2009;302:412-423. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.1063
all_cause_mortality.notes: The Erqou meta-analysis found Lp(a) associations were exclusive to vascular outcomes: no statistically significant association with cancer death, non-vascular non-cancer death, or all non-vascular death. There is therefore no usable all-cause mortality HR; cell left null/qualitative honestly rather than fabricated.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 3.5-fold (1 SD on log scale) higher Lp(a), coronary heart disease, adjusted for conventional risk factors
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
cvd.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration / Erqou meta-analysis of 36 prospective studies (initially healthy populations).
cvd.citation: Erqou S et al. Lipoprotein(a) concentration and the risk of coronary heart disease, stroke, and nonvascular mortality. JAMA 2009;302:412-423. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.1063
cvd.notes: Erqou reported a continuous per-3.5-fold (per log-SD) RR of 1.13 (1.09-1.18) for CHD and 1.10 (1.02-1.18) for ischemic stroke. Causal: strong Mendelian randomization evidence (Burgess et al. JAMA Cardiol 2018) - LPA variants predict CAD proportional to Lp(a) level. Risk is approximately log-linear and continuous; replaces the earlier Women's Health Study quintile cell with a native per-log-SD meta-analytic estimate.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher genetically predicted log Lp(a), Alzheimer's disease, Mendelian randomization
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
dementia.study: Mendelian randomization of Lp(a) and Alzheimer's disease (54,162 European individuals).
dementia.citation: Larsson SC et al. Causal effect of Lp(a) level on ischemic stroke and Alzheimer disease: a Mendelian randomization study. Stroke 2020;51:2455-2461. doi:10.1161/STROKEAHA.119.026872
dementia.notes: MR found a small inverse association of genetically predicted Lp(a) with Alzheimer's disease (OR 0.94 per SD log Lp(a)). The effect is small, not replicated in UK Biobank (null, possibly underpowered), and biologically uncertain; observational dementia associations attenuated after accounting for competing mortality. Treat as exploratory; OR used as a risk-ratio proxy.

modifiability.intervention: Largely non-modifiable by lifestyle or diet. PCSK9 inhibitors lower Lp(a) ~20-25%; niacin ~20-30%; targeted antisense/siRNA agents (pelacarsen, olpasiran) lower it 80-90% but cardiovascular outcome trials are ongoing. Lipoprotein apheresis lowers it acutely in severe cases.
modifiability.effect_size: Diet/exercise: essentially no effect. PCSK9i: ~20-25%. Investigational RNA therapies: 80-90% (outcomes not yet proven).
modifiability.timeframe: Lifestyle: no meaningful change. Drug therapy: weeks.
modifiability.citation: Pelacarsen/olpasiran phase 2 trials (Tsimikas 2020; O'Donoghue 2022); HORIZON outcomes trial ongoing.

profile.mechanism: Non-HDL-C (total cholesterol minus HDL-C) captures the cholesterol carried by ALL atherogenic lipoproteins - LDL, VLDL, IDL, remnants, and Lp(a). It is a more complete measure of atherogenic cholesterol burden than LDL-C, especially when triglycerides are elevated, and requires no fasting or extra assay.

measurement.method: Calculated from a standard lipid panel (total cholesterol minus HDL-C); no fasting required; no additional cost beyond the panel.

profile.signal_quality_note: Derived from two well-standardized, low-variability measurements; valid non-fasting; not biased at high triglycerides as calculated LDL-C is.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual with lipid panel

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: U-shaped association of non-HDL-C with all-cause mortality; no usable linear per-SD risk summary
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: non-linear U-shaped association; no defensible linear per-SD summary
all_cause_mortality.study: UK Biobank systematic analysis of serum lipids and all-cause/cause-specific mortality (and supporting NHANES/Frontiers cohorts).
all_cause_mortality.citation: Systematic analysis of relationships between serum lipids with all-cause and cause-specific mortality: UK Biobank and Women's Health Initiative. Clin Nutr 2025;47:xxx. doi:10.1016/j.clnu.2025.02.017
all_cause_mortality.notes: Like LDL-C, non-HDL-C shows a U-shaped relation to all-cause mortality (NHANES: elevated risk at both <100 and >=200 mg/dL; UK Biobank systematic analysis found most lipid-mortality associations inverse, driven by reverse causation). A single linear per-SD HR is not meaningful; flagged unconvertible. CVD/causal evidence for non-HDL-C remains strong despite the U-shaped all-cause-mortality curve.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD (43 mg/dL) higher non-HDL-C, coronary heart disease, fully adjusted
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration, individual-participant meta-analysis of 68 prospective studies.
cvd.citation: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration (Di Angelantonio E et al.). Major lipids, apolipoproteins, and risk of vascular disease. JAMA 2009;302:1993-2000. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.1619
cvd.notes: ERFC per-SD HR (1 SD non-HDL-C = 43 mg/dL); non-HDL-C and apoB were the most predictive lipid markers in fully adjusted analyses. The Multinational Cardiovascular Risk Consortium (Brunner et al., Lancet 2019; 524,444 individuals, 44 cohorts) confirmed a strong graded long-term association of non-HDL-C with ASCVD. Strongly causal by extension of LDL-C/apoB evidence. Earlier dataset value (1.59 from Sniderman 2011) replaced with the ERFC native per-SD figure.

modifiability.intervention: Statins, ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors; diet (reduced saturated fat, fiber); triglyceride-lowering lifestyle changes also reduce non-HDL-C.
modifiability.effect_size: High-intensity statin lowers non-HDL-C ~45-50%; further reductions with add-on agents.
modifiability.citation: CTT Collaboration statin meta-analyses; non-HDL-C is a guideline secondary treatment target.

profile.mechanism: Remnant cholesterol is the cholesterol carried by triglyceride-rich lipoprotein remnants (VLDL, IDL, chylomicron remnants), calculated as total cholesterol minus LDL-C minus HDL-C. Remnant particles penetrate and are retained in the arterial wall and also drive inflammation; Mendelian randomization implicates remnant cholesterol as causal for ischemic heart disease.

measurement.method: Calculated from standard lipid panel (total cholesterol minus HDL-C minus LDL-C); can also be directly measured. Non-fasting samples capture remnant burden well.

profile.signal_quality_note: Derived value; inherits triglyceride variability and depends on accuracy of the LDL-C estimate, but non-fasting measurement is a strength.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + periodic with lipid panel

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher remnant cholesterol, all-cause mortality, fully adjusted, general population (NHANES III)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
all_cause_mortality.study: NHANES III nationally representative cohort, remnant cholesterol and long-term mortality.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Long-term association of remnant cholesterol with all-cause and cardiovascular disease mortality: a nationally representative cohort study. Front Cardiovasc Med 2024;11:1286091. doi:10.3389/fcvm.2024.1286091
all_cause_mortality.notes: Native per-SD HR 1.17 (1.01-1.36) for all-cause mortality in a general-population cohort with 26.6-year follow-up. Replaces the earlier IHD-only categorical cell (Jepsen 2016, HR 1.6 extreme percentiles) with a general-population per-SD estimate. Copenhagen primary-care data show a linear rise (HR ~1.6 at 3 vs 0.9 mmol/L). Observational; remnant-C is causal for IHD by MR.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 mmol/L (39 mg/dL) higher non-fasting remnant cholesterol, ischemic heart disease, observational
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 0.5 (Copenhagen General Population Study non-fasting remnant cholesterol distribution (~mean 0.7-0.8 mmol/L, SD ~0.5 mmol/L)); k = SD/unit = 0.5

conversion.sd_source: Copenhagen General Population Study non-fasting remnant cholesterol distribution (~mean 0.7-0.8 mmol/L, SD ~0.5 mmol/L)

cvd.study: Copenhagen City Heart Study / Copenhagen General Population Study; observational + Mendelian randomization.
cvd.citation: Varbo A, Nordestgaard BG et al. Remnant cholesterol as a causal risk factor for ischemic heart disease. J Am Coll Cardiol 2013;61:427-436. doi:10.1016/j.jacc.2012.08.1026
cvd.notes: Causal: this landmark Copenhagen study showed observational AND genetic (MR) associations of remnant cholesterol with ischemic heart disease. The per-mmol/L HR is converted to per-SD using a population SD of ~0.5 mmol/L for non-fasting remnant-C; remnant-C is right-skewed so the conversion is approximate. Directionally robust and causal.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: highest vs lowest quartile of remnant cholesterol, incident all-cause dementia
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
dementia.study: South Korean National Health Insurance Service nationwide population-based cohort.
dementia.citation: Heo JH et al. Association of remnant cholesterol with risk of dementia: a nationwide population-based cohort study in South Korea. Lancet Healthy Longev 2024;5:e524-e533. doi:10.1016/S2666-7568(24)00112-0
dementia.notes: Large nationwide cohort: dementia risk rose progressively with remnant-C; Q4 vs Q1 HR 1.11 (all-cause dementia), 1.11 (Alzheimer's), 1.15 (vascular dementia). Risk was stronger in middle-aged and diabetic subgroups. Quartile contrast converted to per-SD; remnant-C is right-skewed so the normal-approx conversion is approximate. Observational; remnant-C is causal for IHD by MR but a dementia causal link is unproven.

modifiability.intervention: Weight loss, reduced carbohydrate and alcohol intake, exercise, glycemic control; statins, fibrates, high-dose omega-3 and (investigationally) ANGPTL3/APOC3 inhibitors lower remnant cholesterol.
modifiability.effect_size: Lifestyle can lower remnant-C substantially via triglyceride reduction; statins lower it ~20-30%.
modifiability.citation: Lifestyle and lipid-lowering intervention studies; MR supports causal benefit of lowering.

profile.mechanism: Oxidized LDL is LDL modified by oxidative stress; it is taken up by macrophage scavenger receptors to form foam cells and triggers endothelial dysfunction and vascular inflammation. Circulating oxLDL is thought to reflect both atherogenic lipoprotein burden and oxidative/inflammatory activity within plaque.

measurement.method: ELISA using monoclonal antibodies (e.g., 4E6 or related) against oxidized epitopes on apoB; specialty lab assay.

profile.signal_quality_note: Different antibody assays measure different epitopes and are not interchangeable; circulating oxLDL correlates strongly with LDL-C/apoB, so independent signal is modest. Not standardized for clinical use.
profile.testing_cadence: research use; not part of routine clinical monitoring

cvd.hr_unit_detail: elevated vs lower circulating oxLDL (pooled high-vs-low dichotomous contrast), atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: median_split contrast; k = 1/1.6 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cvd.study: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 studies of circulating oxLDL and ASCVD.
cvd.citation: Oxidized low-density lipoprotein associates with cardiovascular disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Front Cardiovasc Med 2022;9:1023651. PMID:36727024
cvd.notes: Observational only; no causal/MR evidence. The pooled estimate combines heterogeneous high-vs-low contrasts across studies; treated here as an approximate median-split (k=1/1.60), which is itself an approximation given assay heterogeneity. oxLDL correlates strongly with LDL-C/apoB, so much of the association is not independent. Heterogeneous antibody assays limit comparability.

modifiability.intervention: Statins lower circulating oxLDL (largely by lowering LDL particle number); smoking cessation and improved glycemic control reduce oxidative modification.
modifiability.effect_size: Statins reduce oxLDL roughly in proportion to LDL-C reduction.
modifiability.citation: Statin intervention studies measuring oxLDL as a secondary endpoint.

biomarkers.name: Coronary artery calcium score (CAC)

profile.mechanism: CAC quantifies calcified atherosclerotic plaque in the coronary arteries via non-contrast CT. It is a direct measure of accumulated subclinical atherosclerotic disease burden, integrating lifetime exposure to all risk factors; it is among the strongest predictors of future cardiac events and reclassifies risk beyond standard risk scores.

measurement.method: Non-contrast gated cardiac CT; calcified plaque quantified by the Agatston method; low radiation dose (~1 mSv).

profile.signal_quality_note: Highly reproducible imaging measure with low scan-to-scan variability; directly visualizes disease burden rather than a circulating proxy. Main limitation: detects only calcified plaque, can miss early non-calcified plaque.
confounders: statin use can increase plaque calcification density
profile.testing_cadence: one-time for risk stratification in intermediate-risk adults; repeat scanning generally not recommended at short intervals

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: CAC >=400 vs CAC = 0, all-cause mortality (graded: CAC 1-99 HR ~1.5, 100-399 HR ~1.8)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
all_cause_mortality.study: Long-term observational CAC cohorts (e.g., CAC Consortium and related cohorts) with median follow-up ~11 years.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Long-term CAC and all-cause mortality cohort studies, e.g., 22-year follow-up cohort PMID:31278061 and CAC Consortium analyses.
all_cause_mortality.notes: Graded mortality risk by CAC category vs CAC=0: ~1.5 (1-99), ~1.8 (100-399), ~2.6 (>=400). MESA reported HR ~1.94 (1.57-2.41) for CAC >=1000 vs CAC=0. CAC=0 ('power of zero') marks very low mortality. CAC strata are clinical disease-burden categories on a heavily right-skewed score, treated as categorical and shown in a separate band.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: CAC >300 vs CAC = 0, incident coronary heart disease (MESA); graded: CAC 1-100 HR ~3.9, 101-300 HR ~7.1
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
cvd.study: Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA), prospective cohort, CAC and incident CHD.
cvd.citation: Detrano R et al. Coronary calcium as a predictor of coronary events in four racial or ethnic groups (MESA). N Engl J Med 2008;358:1336-1345.
cvd.notes: MESA reported strongly graded HRs for incident CHD: ~3.89 (CAC 1-100), ~7.08 (101-300), ~6.84 (>300) vs CAC=0. A meta-analysis of younger adults found CAC>100 HR ~6.57 (3.23-13.36) for MACE. CAC is among the strongest observational predictors of CVD and improves risk reclassification, but is a disease marker rather than a modifiable factor; treated as categorical.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: CAC >=400 vs CAC = 0, incident lung and colorectal cancer (total/all-site cancer NOT significantly associated)
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
cancer.study: Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA), prospective cohort, CAC and incident cancer.
cancer.citation: Handy CE et al. (MESA). Coronary artery calcium is associated with increased risk for lung and colorectal cancer in men and women. PMC9016360.
cancer.notes: CAC >=400 vs CAC=0 predicted incident lung+colorectal cancer (HR 2.0; log(CAC+1) continuous HR ~1.1/unit) but was NOT associated with total cancer or hormone-related cancers. Likely reflects shared risk factors (smoking) rather than causation. Site-specific signal only; treated as categorical (Agatston strata).

dementia.hr_unit_detail: CAC >=1001 vs CAC = 0, incident dementia (graded: CAC 1-400 HR ~1.23, 401-1000 HR ~1.35)
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
dementia.study: Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA), prospective cohort, CAC and incident dementia.
dementia.citation: Fujiyoshi A et al. Coronary artery calcium and risk of dementia in MESA. Circ Cardiovasc Imaging 2017;10:e005564. doi:10.1161/CIRCIMAGING.116.005564
dementia.notes: MESA found a graded association of CAC with incident dementia: ~+23% (CAC 1-400), ~+35% (401-1000), ~+71% (>=1001) vs CAC=0, independent of vascular risk factors and APOE4. Association attenuated to non-significance after adjusting for interim CVD but remained after excluding interim stroke. CIs are approximate; treated as categorical (Agatston strata).

modifiability.intervention: CAC reflects established calcified plaque and does not regress; the score itself cannot be lowered. Statins paradoxically increase calcified plaque density while stabilizing plaque. The actionable response is aggressive risk-factor and lipid management triggered by an elevated score.
modifiability.effect_size: CAC score is essentially fixed/non-regressing; clinical value is in guiding intensity of preventive therapy.
modifiability.citation: Statin-CAC progression studies; guideline use of CAC to guide therapy intensity.

biomarkers.comparability_note: Coronary artery calcium has a large point mass at zero and a long skewed tail, and risk rises across graded clinical strata rather than along a smooth slope — it is reported by CAC category, not per SD.

profile.mechanism: cIMT measures the combined thickness of the intima and media layers of the carotid artery wall by ultrasound. Increased cIMT reflects subclinical atherosclerosis and arterial aging and is associated with future stroke and myocardial infarction, though it adds limited incremental predictive value over standard risk factors.

measurement.method: B-mode carotid ultrasound; standardized measurement of mean common carotid artery wall thickness; operator-dependent.

profile.signal_quality_note: Operator- and protocol-dependent; measurement definitions vary (near vs far wall, segments included), limiting comparability. Reproducible within standardized protocols.
profile.testing_cadence: one-time for risk assessment; serial measurement of progression has not proven clinically useful

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: highest vs lowest tertile of carotid IMT, all-cause mortality, adjusted for risk factors, plaque and CRP
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: tertile contrast; k = 1/2.18 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
all_cause_mortality.study: Cardiovascular Health Study, prospective cohort of older community-dwelling adults.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Cao JJ et al. Association of carotid artery intima-media thickness, plaques, and C-reactive protein with future cardiovascular disease and all-cause mortality (Cardiovascular Health Study). Circulation 2007;116:32-38. doi:10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.106.645606
all_cause_mortality.notes: Highest vs lowest cIMT tertile carried ~54% higher all-cause mortality after adjustment for risk factors, carotid plaque and CRP. Smaller cohorts (e.g., Hokkaido LILAC) report a positive cIMT-all-cause-mortality gradient. Tertile contrast converted to per-SD; the source CI was not captured so the cell is approximate. Observational; cIMT is a subclinical-atherosclerosis marker.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD higher mean common carotid artery IMT, CVD events, age- and sex-adjusted
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Systematic review and meta-analysis of cIMT definitions and future CVD across prospective cohorts.
cvd.citation: Varying Definitions of Carotid Intima-Media Thickness and Future Cardiovascular Disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Am Heart Assoc 2023;12:e031217. doi:10.1161/JAHA.123.031217
cvd.notes: Per-SD HRs from meta-analyses are consistent: ~1.25-1.36 for CVD events, ~1.25 for MI, ~1.32 for stroke. Observational only - cIMT is a marker of subclinical atherosclerosis; serial regression of cIMT with therapy has not translated into proven event reduction, and it adds modest incremental value over standard risk scores.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1 SD (0.19 mm) higher carotid intima-media thickness, incident dementia, fully adjusted
dementia.study: Atherosclerosis Risk in Communities Neurocognitive Study (ARIC-NCS), prospective cohort.
dementia.citation: Kresge HA et al. Association of carotid intima-media thickness and other carotid ultrasound features with incident dementia in ARIC-NCS. J Am Heart Assoc 2021;10:e020489. doi:10.1161/JAHA.120.020489
dementia.notes: ARIC-NCS reported a native per-SD HR of 1.08 (1.04-1.13) for incident dementia (highest vs lowest quintile HR 1.25, 1.08-1.45). Mid-life cIMT is prospectively associated with dementia, consistent with a vascular contribution to cognitive decline. Observational; cIMT is a subclinical-atherosclerosis marker, not a treatment target.

modifiability.intervention: Statins, blood pressure control, and lifestyle changes slow or modestly reverse cIMT progression.
modifiability.effect_size: Statins can slow progression and produce small regression of cIMT; absolute changes are small.
modifiability.citation: Meta-analyses of statin and antihypertensive trials measuring cIMT progression (surrogate endpoint).

biomarkers.name: ApoE genotype

profile.mechanism: APOE has three common alleles (e2, e3, e4) encoding apolipoprotein E, which mediates clearance of triglyceride-rich and remnant lipoproteins. The e4 allele raises LDL-C and remnant levels and modestly increases coronary heart disease risk; it is also the strongest common genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease. e2 lowers LDL-C but can predispose to type III hyperlipoproteinemia.

measurement.method: Genotyping of the two APOE SNPs (rs429358, rs7412) by PCR or array; a single test, results are lifelong.

profile.signal_quality_note: Genotype is fixed and measured with essentially no biological or analytic variability; the test is definitive once performed.
confounders: phenotypic expression modified by environment, sex, ancestry
profile.testing_cadence: once in a lifetime (genotype is fixed)

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: APOE e4 heterozygote vs e3/e3, all-cause mortality (e4/e4 homozygotes HR 1.59, 1.24-2.06)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
all_cause_mortality.study: Pooled analysis of two population-based cohorts (Three-City study + Whitehall II).
all_cause_mortality.citation: Pooled analyses of APOE4 and all-cause mortality. Lancet Healthy Longev 2024;5:e413-e421. doi:10.1016/S2666-7568(24)00066-7
all_cause_mortality.notes: Replaces the earlier single small elderly-cohort cell with a pooled two-cohort meta-analytic estimate: e4 heterozygotes HR 1.16 (1.07-1.26), e4/e4 homozygotes HR 1.59 (1.24-2.06) vs e3/e3. Excess mortality was only partly mediated by dementia. Genotype is a fixed categorical/causal exposure; remains categorical.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: e4 carrier vs non-carrier (predominantly e3/e3), incident coronary heart disease
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
cvd.study: EPIC-Norfolk prospective cohort, English men and women aged 40-79.
cvd.citation: APOE genotype and coronary heart disease in EPIC-Norfolk; reviewed in Apolipoprotein E Genotype and Cardiovascular Diseases in the Elderly, PMC2908458.
cvd.notes: e4 carriage is associated with a modest increase in CHD risk (~6-46% across populations); estimate cited is from EPIC-Norfolk. The effect is partly mediated by higher LDL-C and remnant cholesterol. e2 is generally associated with lower LDL-C and lower CHD risk. Genotype is causal/genetic but effect on CVD is small.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: approx odds/risk ratio for one e4 allele (heterozygote) vs e3/e3 for late-onset Alzheimer's disease; e4/e4 homozygotes carry roughly 8-12x risk
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
dementia.study: Meta-analyses of APOE and Alzheimer's disease (e.g., AlzGene, Farrer 1997 and subsequent consortia).
dementia.citation: Farrer LA et al. Effects of age, sex, and ethnicity on the association between APOE genotype and Alzheimer disease. JAMA 1997;278:1349-1356; and subsequent meta-analyses.
dementia.notes: APOE e4 is the strongest common genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's: roughly 3x for one e4 allele and 8-12x (some estimates higher) for e4/e4 homozygotes vs e3/e3; e2 is protective. Magnitude varies by ancestry (weaker in some African-ancestry populations). This is a genetic/causal association. Cited CI is approximate/illustrative for the heterozygote estimate.

modifiability.intervention: Genotype is fixed and cannot be changed. e4 carriers benefit from earlier and more intensive management of modifiable risk factors (LDL-C lowering, blood pressure, exercise, sleep) for both cardiovascular and dementia risk.
modifiability.effect_size: Not applicable - genotype is immutable; downstream lipid and cognitive risk is partially modifiable.
modifiability.citation: APOE biology; risk-factor modification trials in e4 carriers.

biomarkers.comparability_note: ApoE is a genotype (e2/e3/e4 allele status), not a continuous measurement — there is no standard deviation to standardize against; HRs are e4-carrier group contrasts.

profile.mechanism: VO2max integrates the capacity of the lungs, heart, vasculature and skeletal-muscle mitochondria to deliver and utilize oxygen. It is the single most robust functional predictor of all-cause and cardiovascular mortality, and declining VO2max tracks the loss of physiologic reserve that defines biological aging.

measurement.method: Gold standard: graded maximal exercise test (treadmill or cycle ergometer) with expired-gas analysis (cardiopulmonary exercise testing, CPET). Submaximal estimates and treadmill-time-derived METs are common surrogates in large cohorts.

profile.signal_quality_note: Directly measured CPET VO2max is highly reproducible (test-retest CV ~3-5%). Estimated/submaximal values introduce moderate noise; effort dependence and ceiling at true max are the main measurement issues.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + every 1-2 years

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 1-MET higher maximal aerobic capacity
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 2 (Within-cohort CRF SD ~2 MET (~7 mL/kg/min), consistent with FRIEND registry reference distributions (Kaminsky 2015 Mayo Clin Proc) and large treadmill cohorts.); k = SD/unit = 2

conversion.sd_source: Within-cohort CRF SD ~2 MET (~7 mL/kg/min), consistent with FRIEND registry reference distributions (Kaminsky 2015 Mayo Clin Proc) and large treadmill cohorts.

all_cause_mortality.study: Kodama 2009, JAMA, meta-analysis of prospective cohorts in healthy adults.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Kodama S, et al. Cardiorespiratory fitness as a quantitative predictor of all-cause mortality and cardiovascular events in healthy men and women: a meta-analysis. JAMA. 2009;301(19):2024-2035. doi:10.1001/jama.2009.681. PMID:19454641
all_cause_mortality.notes: Per-1-MET RR for all-cause mortality 0.87 (0.84-0.90). Mandsager 2018 (JAMA Netw Open, n=122,007, median 8.4y) found low vs elite fitness adjusted HR 5.04 (4.10-6.20) with no upper limit of benefit. A per-1-SD CPET-VO2 estimate of HR ~0.82 (0.69-0.97) is reported in heart-failure CPET cohorts; the per-MET meta-analytic estimate is retained as the strongest population-level figure. Observational; 2-sample Mendelian randomization found VO2max not robustly causally associated with longevity, suggesting residual confounding/reverse causation inflates observational estimates.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1-MET higher maximal aerobic capacity (CHD/CVD events)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 2 (Within-cohort CRF SD ~2 MET (~7 mL/kg/min), FRIEND registry reference distributions (Kaminsky 2015 Mayo Clin Proc).); k = SD/unit = 2

conversion.sd_source: Within-cohort CRF SD ~2 MET (~7 mL/kg/min), FRIEND registry reference distributions (Kaminsky 2015 Mayo Clin Proc).

cvd.study: Kodama 2009, JAMA, meta-analysis of prospective cohorts.
cvd.notes: Per-1-MET RR for combined CHD/CVD events 0.85 (0.82-0.88). Consistent with later dose-response meta-analyses. Observational.

cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 2 (Within-cohort CRF SD ~2 MET (~7 mL/kg/min), FRIEND registry reference distributions (Kaminsky 2015 Mayo Clin Proc).); k = SD/unit = 2

conversion.sd_source: Within-cohort CRF SD ~2 MET (~7 mL/kg/min), FRIEND registry reference distributions (Kaminsky 2015 Mayo Clin Proc).

cancer.study: Schmid & Leitzmann 2015, Annals of Oncology, systematic review/meta-analysis of CRF and cancer mortality; incidence HR from CRF-cancer-in-women cohort literature.
cancer.citation: Schmid D, Leitzmann MF. Cardiorespiratory fitness as predictor of cancer mortality: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Oncol. 2015;26(2):272-278. doi:10.1093/annonc/mdu250. PMID:25009011
cancer.notes: Schmid 2015 meta-analysis (6 prospective studies, 71,654 individuals, 2,002 cancer deaths, median 16.4y) showed a strong graded inverse association of CRF with total cancer mortality. The per-1-MET incidence HR 0.80 is drawn from a CRF-cancer-in-women cohort and is approximate. Observational; site-specific effects vary (strongest for lung, colorectal).

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1-SD higher cardiorespiratory fitness, incident all-cause dementia
dementia.study: Cheng 2023-type analyses of objectively measured CRF and incident dementia; per-1-SD increment associated with reduced all-dementia risk.
dementia.citation: Cheng Y, et al. Cardiorespiratory fitness and risk of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias among American veterans. Alzheimers Dement. 2023; companion per-SD analyses report incident all-dementia IRR ~0.81 (0.73-0.89) and Alzheimer's IRR ~0.83 (0.72-0.97) per 1-SD higher CRF.
dementia.notes: Per-1-SD CRF: incident all-dementia IRR 0.81 (0.73-0.89); replaces the earlier Defina 2013 quintile contrast (highest vs lowest quintile HR 0.64, 0.54-0.77) with a native per-SD estimate. The Defina Cooper Center study (n=19,458, median 25y, 1,659 cases) remains the landmark long-follow-up cohort. Observational; reverse causation over long follow-up partially mitigated by midlife/baseline measurement.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: higher cardiorespiratory fitness associated with lower incident frailty; no measured-VO2max per-SD HR available
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: no directly-measured-VO2max per-SD incident-frailty HR exists; the closest evidence uses physical-activity exposure rather than CPET VO2max, so it cannot be standardized to a per-SD VO2max hazard ratio
frailty.study: Prospective cohorts consistently show baseline physical activity / fitness predicts incident frailty (e.g. Whitehall II: >=2.5 h/week activity at age 50, incident frailty HR 0.66, 0.54-0.81; trajectory cohorts HR 0.42-0.70 for sustained high vs low activity). VO2max correlates cross-sectionally with Fried frailty score (r ~ -0.40).
frailty.citation: Physical activity and incident frailty literature, e.g. Whitehall II (Bouillon 2013); 7-year activity-trajectory cohorts. No prospective cohort reports a per-SD hazard ratio for directly measured VO2max and incident frailty.
frailty.notes: Low fitness/activity is a strong, consistent predictor of incident frailty, and VO2max is mechanistically central to physiologic reserve, but the available hard-outcome HRs use physical-activity (not CPET VO2max) exposure. Left unconvertible/qualitative rather than borrowing an activity HR onto a fitness scale. Direction is firmly protective. Tier C; reverse causation (subclinical decline lowers fitness) is a concern.

modifiability.intervention: Structured aerobic / endurance training, especially moderate-to-vigorous continuous exercise and high-intensity interval training.
modifiability.effect_size: Typically 10-25% increase in VO2max with 3-6 months of training; larger relative gains in deconditioned and older individuals, attenuated in already-fit and very old.
modifiability.timeframe: 8-12 weeks for measurable gains; 6 months for substantial improvement.
modifiability.citation: Numerous RCT meta-analyses of aerobic and HIIT training (e.g., Bacon 2013 PLoS One; Milanovic 2015 Sports Med) consistently show ~10-25% VO2max improvement.

profile.mechanism: Ventilatory thresholds mark the exercise intensities at which ventilation rises disproportionately as metabolic acidosis develops (VT1 ~aerobic/gas-exchange threshold, VT2 ~respiratory compensation point). They index submaximal mitochondrial oxidative capacity and the workload sustainable without progressive acidosis, and they decline with age and deconditioning.

measurement.method: Identified during cardiopulmonary exercise testing from ventilatory equivalents and gas-exchange slopes (V-slope method).

profile.signal_quality_note: Threshold determination is somewhat operator- and method-dependent with inter-rater variability; physiologically meaningful but harder to standardize than VO2max.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual (with CPET)

cvd.hr_unit_detail: low vs high anaerobic threshold in heart failure / preoperative populations
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: no usable summary HR; anaerobic-threshold prognosis reported heterogeneously across clinical CPET studies without a pooled estimate or SD
cvd.study: Multiple clinical CPET studies in heart failure and preoperative risk stratification report anaerobic-threshold-based prognosis, but no single landmark meta-analysis with a clean HR.
cvd.citation: No single definitive citation; anaerobic threshold prognostic value summarized in CPET guideline statements (e.g., Guazzi 2016 EACPR/AHA scientific statement on CPET).
cvd.notes: Anaerobic/ventilatory threshold predicts perioperative and heart-failure outcomes, but hard-outcome evidence in general aging populations is thin and largely subsumed by VO2max. No verified HR; effort to find a per-SD or per-unit population estimate was unsuccessful, so the continuous exposure is left unconvertible. Tier C, intermediate physiologic marker.

modifiability.intervention: Aerobic endurance training, particularly threshold and tempo work.
modifiability.effect_size: Ventilatory thresholds shift to higher absolute and relative workloads with training, often improving more than VO2max in percentage terms.
modifiability.citation: Endurance training trials consistently show upward shifts in ventilatory/lactate thresholds (general exercise physiology literature).

profile.mechanism: RMR is the energy expended at rest, dominated by organ and lean-tissue mitochondrial activity. Its relationship to aging is complex: low RMR is linked to longevity in cross-species comparisons, yet in humans higher RMR predicts mortality, an association largely confounded by fat mass and subclinical disease.

measurement.method: Indirect calorimetry (ventilated hood) under fasted, rested conditions; predictive equations are common but less accurate.

profile.signal_quality_note: Strongly determined by lean and fat mass; once body composition is accounted for, residual RMR signal is small and easily confounded by recent food, sleep, temperature, thyroid status and illness.
profile.testing_cadence: not routinely tracked; baseline if metabolic assessment indicated

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: higher BMR/24h energy expenditure associated with increased mortality
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: direction reported but no consistent per-SD HR magnitude or usable SD published; cannot be standardized
all_cause_mortality.study: Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging and Pima Indian cohorts: higher BMR independently predicted increased natural mortality.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Jumpertz R, et al. Higher energy expenditure in humans predicts natural mortality. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2011;96(6):E972-E976; Ruggiero C, et al. High basal metabolic rate is a risk factor for mortality: the Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging. J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. 2008;63(7):698-706
all_cause_mortality.notes: Direction is paradoxical: higher RMR predicts higher mortality in human cohorts (BLSA, Pima), interpreted as a marker of subclinical disease/inflammation and accelerated aging. Speakman & Westerterp 2014 showed the low-RMR/longer-lifespan association largely disappears after adjusting for fat mass. Re-search confirmed no consistent per-SD HR magnitude is reported; the cell is genuinely thin and confounded. Left unconvertible, tier C.

modifiability.intervention: Resistance training to increase lean mass; RMR otherwise tightly regulated and not a useful intervention target.
modifiability.effect_size: Small (a few percent) changes via lean-mass gain; adaptive thermogenesis lowers RMR with weight loss.
modifiability.citation: Body-composition and weight-change literature on RMR adaptation.

profile.mechanism: The respiratory exchange ratio and maximal fat oxidation reflect the balance between fat and carbohydrate combustion and the ability to switch fuels (metabolic flexibility). Impaired fat oxidation and blunted metabolic flexibility are features of insulin resistance and mitochondrial dysfunction, mechanistically tied to cardiometabolic aging.

measurement.method: Indirect calorimetry at rest (RER) and during graded exercise to derive maximal fat oxidation rate and FATmax intensity.

profile.signal_quality_note: FATmax/MFO have well-documented reproducibility problems (test protocol, prior diet, glycogen status, day-to-day variability); RER is highly sensitive to recent carbohydrate intake.
profile.testing_cadence: not routinely tracked

modifiability.intervention: Endurance and FATmax-intensity exercise training; weight loss; low-carbohydrate dietary periodization.
modifiability.effect_size: Maximal fat oxidation and metabolic flexibility improve meaningfully with training; FATmax-intensity training programs reduce body fat, visceral fat and HOMA-IR.
modifiability.citation: Systematic review/meta-analysis of FATmax-intensity exercise training for cardiometabolic health in overweight/obesity (Sports Med / Med Sci Sports Exerc literature, 2024-2025).

profile.mechanism: Exercise economy is the oxygen (energy) cost of locomotion at a fixed submaximal speed. Poorer walking economy means greater relative effort for daily tasks, contributes to fatigue and activity avoidance, and rises with age - a determinant of mobility decline and loss of independence.

measurement.method: Indirect calorimetry during steady-state submaximal treadmill walking or running at standardized speeds.

profile.signal_quality_note: Reasonably reproducible within a session, but absolute values depend on test speed, footwear, gait mechanics and familiarization; cross-study comparability is limited.
profile.testing_cadence: not routinely tracked

frailty.hr_unit_detail: higher oxygen cost of walking associated with greater mobility decline / fatigue
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: association documented but no quantified hard-outcome HR or usable SD; cannot be standardized
frailty.study: Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging and related work links a high energetic cost of walking to slower gait, fatigue and mobility limitation in older adults.
frailty.citation: Schrack JA, et al. The role of energetic cost in the age-related slowing of gait speed. J Am Geriatr Soc. 2012;60(10):1811-1816. PMID:23035768
frailty.notes: Poor walking economy is associated with the mobility-limitation/frailty pathway, but re-search confirmed no verified hard-outcome HR exists. Left unconvertible, tier C. Largely a mechanistic/intermediate marker.

modifiability.intervention: Endurance training, strength and gait training, weight loss.
modifiability.effect_size: Walking/running economy improves modestly with training and weight reduction; gains are smaller and slower than VO2max changes.
modifiability.citation: Exercise physiology literature on economy adaptation; aging mobility studies.

profile.mechanism: The lactate threshold is the exercise intensity above which blood lactate accumulates faster than it can be cleared, reflecting the balance between glycolytic flux and mitochondrial oxidative capacity. A higher threshold and faster lactate clearance indicate better skeletal-muscle oxidative function, which declines with age and deconditioning.

measurement.method: Serial capillary (fingertip/earlobe) blood lactate sampling during a graded exercise test; thresholds identified from the lactate-workload curve.

profile.signal_quality_note: Lactate values are reproducible but threshold definitions vary widely (fixed 2/4 mmol/L vs individualized methods), and results depend on protocol, prior diet and glycogen status.
profile.testing_cadence: not routinely tracked; used in athletic monitoring

modifiability.intervention: Endurance training, especially threshold-intensity and high-volume aerobic training.
modifiability.effect_size: Lactate threshold shifts substantially to higher workloads with training; often the most trainable submaximal aerobic parameter.
modifiability.citation: Endurance-training literature consistently shows rightward shift of the lactate curve.

profile.mechanism: Grip strength is a simple, reliable proxy for total-body muscle strength and overall physiologic reserve. Low grip strength reflects sarcopenia, neuromuscular decline and systemic frailty, and is one of the strongest single-measure predictors of mortality and disability in aging.

measurement.method: Maximal isometric force with a calibrated hand dynamometer (e.g., Jamar), best of multiple trials, typically dominant hand.

profile.signal_quality_note: Highly reproducible with standardized protocol and calibrated dynamometer; main variability sources are posture, instructions and effort, all controllable.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 5 kg lower grip strength
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 5; population SD 8 (Within-sex adult handgrip SD ~8 kg, from UK Biobank normative values (Dodds 2014 J Am Geriatr Soc / UK Biobank reference distributions) and the British normative pooled data (Dodds 2014 PLoS One).); k = SD/unit = 1.6

conversion.sd_source: Within-sex adult handgrip SD ~8 kg, from UK Biobank normative values (Dodds 2014 J Am Geriatr Soc / UK Biobank reference distributions) and the British normative pooled data (Dodds 2014 PLoS One).

all_cause_mortality.study: Leong 2015, Lancet, PURE prospective cohort across 17 countries.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Leong DP, et al. Prognostic value of grip strength: findings from the Prospective Urban Rural Epidemiology (PURE) study. Lancet. 2015;386(9990):266-273. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(14)62000-6. PMID:25982160
all_cause_mortality.notes: PURE: per 5 kg lower grip strength, all-cause mortality HR 1.16 (1.13-1.20). Closely matched by UK Biobank (Celis-Morales 2018 BMJ, n=502,293): per-5-kg HR men 1.16 (1.15-1.17), women 1.20 (1.17-1.23). Tier A given concordant very large cohorts. Grip strength outperformed systolic BP as a mortality predictor in PURE. Observational; partly reflects reverse causation.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 5 kg lower grip strength, cardiovascular mortality
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 5; population SD 8 (Within-sex adult handgrip SD ~8 kg, UK Biobank normative values (Dodds 2014) and British pooled normative data.); k = SD/unit = 1.6

conversion.sd_source: Within-sex adult handgrip SD ~8 kg, UK Biobank normative values (Dodds 2014) and British pooled normative data.

cvd.study: Leong 2015, Lancet, PURE prospective cohort across 17 countries.
cvd.citation: Leong DP, et al. Prognostic value of grip strength: findings from the Prospective Urban Rural Epidemiology (PURE) study. Lancet. 2015;386(9990):266-273. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(14)62000-6. PMID:25982160
cvd.notes: PURE: CVD mortality HR 1.17 (1.11-1.24) per 5 kg lower; MI 1.07, stroke 1.09 per 5 kg lower. UK Biobank (Celis-Morales 2018) found CVD mortality HR 1.22 (men) and 1.19 (women) per 5 kg lower. Observational.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: per 5 kg lower grip strength, cancer mortality (men); 1.17 in women
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 5; population SD 8 (Within-sex adult handgrip SD ~8 kg, UK Biobank normative values (Dodds 2014).); k = SD/unit = 1.6

conversion.sd_source: Within-sex adult handgrip SD ~8 kg, UK Biobank normative values (Dodds 2014).

cancer.study: Celis-Morales 2018, BMJ, UK Biobank prospective cohort.
cancer.citation: Celis-Morales CA, et al. Associations of grip strength with cardiovascular, respiratory, and cancer outcomes and all cause mortality: prospective cohort study of half a million UK Biobank participants. BMJ. 2018;361:k1651. doi:10.1136/bmj.k1651. PMID:29739772
cancer.notes: Cancer mortality per 5 kg lower grip: men 1.10 (1.07-1.13), women 1.17 (1.13-1.21). Likely partly reverse causation (occult cancer reducing strength); association weaker than for CVD.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1-SD lower grip strength, incident dementia (pooled)
dementia.study: Jia 2021, Front Aging Neurosci, systematic review/meta-analysis of 15 longitudinal cohort studies.
dementia.citation: Jia S, et al. Grip strength and the risk of cognitive decline and dementia: a systematic review and meta-analysis of longitudinal cohort studies. Front Aging Neurosci. 2021;13:625551. doi:10.3389/fnagi.2021.625551. PMID:33613270
dementia.notes: Pooled HR per 1-SD lower grip strength: dementia 1.54 (1.32-1.79); cognitive decline 1.99 (1.71-2.32). This is a native per-SD pooled estimate (the earlier 'lowest vs highest category' label was incorrect). Observational; reverse causation possible as preclinical dementia reduces strength.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: lowest vs highest grip strength quartile, incident ADL disability (men)
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
frailty.study: Al Snih 2004, Aging Clin Exp Res, Hispanic EPESE 7-year prospective cohort of non-disabled older men.
frailty.citation: Al Snih S, et al. Hand grip strength and incident ADL disability in elderly Mexican Americans over a seven-year period. Aging Clin Exp Res. 2004;16(6):481-486. PMID:15739601
frailty.notes: Low grip strength is itself a core component of the Fried frailty phenotype, so it is partly definitional. HR 1.90 (1.14-3.17) for incident ADL disability, lowest vs highest quartile (men). Converted from a quartile contrast rather than left categorical because grip strength is a continuous exposure. Tier B; abundant consistent literature links low grip to incident frailty and disability.

modifiability.intervention: Progressive resistance training, including grip- and forearm-specific loading.
modifiability.effect_size: Resistance training increases grip and overall strength by ~10-30% in older adults within months; among the most trainable functional markers.
modifiability.citation: Resistance-training RCT meta-analyses in older adults consistently show meaningful strength gains.

biomarkers.name: Lower-body strength / power relative to body mass

profile.mechanism: Lower-body strength and power relative to body mass govern the ability to rise from a chair, climb stairs and recover balance. Power (force x velocity) declines faster than strength with age and is a strong determinant of mobility, fall risk and loss of independence.

measurement.method: Leg-press 1-RM or isokinetic dynamometry; field surrogates include the 5- or 30-second sit-to-stand and sit-to-stand power estimated from chair-rise time, height and body mass.

profile.signal_quality_note: Laboratory dynamometry is reproducible; field power estimates from chair-rise are practical but introduce estimation error and depend on technique and motivation.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: low vs normal relative sit-to-stand power, all-cause mortality (men); 2.04 in women
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
all_cause_mortality.study: Toledo Study for Healthy Aging, prospective cohort of community-dwelling older adults.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Alcazar J, et al. Low relative sit-to-stand power is associated with history of falls and fractures, prospective hospitalization, and all-cause mortality in older adults from the Toledo Study for Healthy Aging. J Sport Health Sci. 2024 (epub). doi:10.1016/j.jshs.2025.101034
all_cause_mortality.notes: Low relative sit-to-stand power: HR 1.57 (1.26-1.97) men, 2.04 (1.51-2.74) women for all-cause mortality. The 'low vs normal' contrast uses validated clinical cutoffs, not symmetric quantile groups, so it cannot be converted to per-SD with a fixed spread; kept categorical. Re-search did not yield a per-SD lower-body-power mortality HR in a general population. Follow-up duration not confirmed from abstract. Tier B. Observational.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: higher vs lower knee-extension (lower-body) strength, cardiovascular/all-cause mortality
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
cvd.study: Garcia-Hermoso 2018, Br J Sports Med, systematic review/meta-analysis of muscular strength and mortality (knee-extension strength subset).
cvd.citation: Garcia-Hermoso A, et al. Muscular strength as a predictor of all-cause mortality in an apparently healthy population: a systematic review and meta-analysis of data from approximately 2 million men and women. Arch Phys Med Rehabil / Br J Sports Med 2018. PMID:29425700
cvd.notes: Higher knee-extension (lower-body) strength: 14% lower mortality (HR 0.86, 0.80-0.93). The pooled estimate aggregates heterogeneous higher-vs-lower strength contrasts, so it is kept categorical rather than converted. CVD-specific events in general populations are less cleanly reported: in coronary-disease cohorts higher quadriceps strength predicted lower CV death (HR 0.66 per 10% body-weight increment). No general-population per-SD lower-body-strength incident-CVD HR was located. Observational.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: low vs normal relative leg/sit-to-stand power, incident falls and fractures
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: low vs normal power group (clinical cutoff); no verified incident-frailty HR available
frailty.study: Toledo Study for Healthy Aging: low relative sit-to-stand power associated with history of falls/fractures and prospective hospitalization.
frailty.citation: Alcazar J, et al. J Sport Health Sci. 2024 (epub). doi:10.1016/j.jshs.2025.101034
frailty.notes: Low lower-body power is strongly associated with falls, fractures and the mobility-disability/frailty pathway, but a verified incident-frailty HR is not available. Effect size left null; tier C.

modifiability.intervention: Progressive resistance training, especially high-velocity / power-oriented training and explosive sit-to-stand exercise.
modifiability.effect_size: Lower-body strength improves 25-100%+ and muscle power improves substantially in older adults with 8-12 weeks of resistance/power training; power-oriented training is more effective than slow heavy training for power gains.
modifiability.citation: Power- and resistance-training RCT meta-analyses in older adults.
