biomarkers.comparability_note: The available HRs contrast a clinically-defined low-strength group against normal — a threshold split of unknown SD width, neither a quantile nor a per-unit estimate, so it cannot be standardized.

biomarkers.name: Muscle mass (appendicular lean mass index)

profile.mechanism: Appendicular lean mass index (limb lean mass / height^2) quantifies skeletal-muscle quantity, the substrate for strength, metabolic glucose disposal and protein reserve. Low muscle mass (sarcopenia) reflects anabolic decline and predicts disability and mortality, though strength predicts outcomes more strongly than mass alone.

measurement.method: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) appendicular lean mass; bioelectrical impedance is a lower-cost surrogate.

profile.signal_quality_note: DEXA lean mass is reproducible (CV ~1-2%), but hydration status affects readings and lean mass includes non-muscle tissue; muscle quality/strength is not captured.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + every 1-2 years

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 1-SD higher appendicular lean mass, all-cause mortality (prefrail/frail older adults)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
all_cause_mortality.study: NHANES III prospective cohort of prefrail/frail older adults aged >=65 (Cawthon-type analysis of appendicular lean mass and mortality).
all_cause_mortality.citation: Appendicular lean mass and mortality among prefrail and frail older adults. J Nutr Health Aging / NHANES III analysis. PMID:28244576. Each SD higher ALM HR 0.50 (0.27-0.92) in fully adjusted models.
all_cause_mortality.notes: Per-1-SD higher appendicular lean mass: all-cause mortality HR 0.50 (0.27-0.92) in fully adjusted models among prefrail/frail older adults (NHANES III, mean ALM 18.9 kg, SD 5.5 kg). The pooled meta-analytic categorical estimate (Liu 2023 PLoS One: low vs normal SMI by DXA HR 1.86, 1.26-2.74; by BIA 1.20, 1.07-1.33) is consistent in direction. Native per-SD figure used here; the prefrail/frail subgroup limits generalizability. Muscle mass alone is a weaker mortality predictor than grip strength. Observational, with reverse causation.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: low vs normal muscle mass, incident cardiovascular events
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
cvd.study: Damluji/Veronese-type meta-analysis of longitudinal studies on sarcopenia, low muscle mass and cardiovascular outcomes.
cvd.citation: Meta-analysis of sarcopenia and cardiovascular events/disease (100 longitudinal studies, ~2.3 million participants): low muscle mass and incident cardiovascular events aRR 1.43 (1.23-1.68); sarcopenia overall aRR 1.63 (1.30-2.04). Published 2023-2026 in the cardiology/sarcopenia literature.
cvd.notes: Low muscle mass is associated with higher incident cardiovascular events (aRR 1.43, 1.23-1.68). The contrast is low-vs-normal by sarcopenia diagnostic cutoffs, so it is kept categorical rather than converted with a fixed quantile spread. A per-SD continuous ALMI-CVD HR in a general population was not located. Observational; muscle mass is a weaker independent CVD predictor than strength/fitness, and confounding by adiposity and frailty is substantial.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: per 20-percentile-point higher appendicular skeletal muscle index, cancer mortality
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit but population SD unavailable — not standardized
cancer.study: NHANES (1999-2006, 2011-2018) prospective cohort linked to the 2018 mortality file; appendicular skeletal muscle index and cancer mortality.
cancer.citation: Low appendicular skeletal muscle mass is associated with the risk of mortality among adults in the United States. Sci Rep. 2025;15. doi:10.1038/s41598-025-94357-8. PMID:40121321
cancer.notes: Higher appendicular skeletal muscle index was associated with lower cancer mortality: per 20-percentile-point increment HR 0.87 (0.78-0.96). Because the exposure is expressed as a percentile rank rather than in kg/m^2, it cannot be defensibly converted to per-SD; left unconvertible. This is cancer mortality (not incidence), in a general population. Tier C; reverse causation likely (occult cancer/cachexia lowers muscle mass).

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1-SD lower appendicular lean mass / BMI ratio, incident dementia (men)
dementia.study: Health ABC Study, prospective cohort of community-dwelling older adults (1,290 men, 1,414 women).
dementia.citation: Hsu B, et al. Appendicular lean mass, grip strength, and the incidence of dementia among older adults in the Health ABC Study. J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. 2023;78(11):2070-2077. doi:10.1093/gerona/glac240. PMID:36548124
dementia.notes: Health ABC: per 1-SD lower appendicular-lean-mass/BMI ratio, incident dementia HR 1.33 (1.07-1.65) in MEN; the association was not significant in women (sex-specific result). Median 9.2-year follow-up; 28-29% developed dementia. A separate sarcopenia meta-analysis is concordant (incident AD HR ~1.50-1.57). Native per-SD estimate; the men-only signal limits generalizability. Observational; reverse causation possible as preclinical dementia reduces lean mass.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: low appendicular lean mass index, incident frailty / sarcopenia
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: low ALMI is a defining diagnostic criterion of sarcopenia/frailty, not an independent continuous predictor with a clean HR
frailty.study: Low ALMI is a defining criterion of sarcopenia (EWGSOP2) and a component of physical frailty pathways.
frailty.citation: Cruz-Jentoft AJ, et al. Sarcopenia: revised European consensus on definition and diagnosis (EWGSOP2). Age Ageing. 2019;48(1):16-31. PMID:30312372
frailty.notes: Low ALMI is partly definitional for sarcopenia/frailty rather than an independent predictor with a clean HR. Effect size left null; tier C. Strength and physical performance carry more independent prognostic weight than mass.

modifiability.intervention: Progressive resistance training, with adequate dietary protein; combined with anabolic stimulus in deficient states.
modifiability.effect_size: Resistance training increases appendicular lean mass by roughly 0.5-2 kg over 3-6 months in older adults; gains are modest in magnitude relative to strength gains.
modifiability.citation: Resistance-training RCT meta-analyses (e.g., Peterson 2011 Ageing Res Rev) show significant lean-mass gains in older adults.

biomarkers.comparability_note: Muscle-mass HRs here use a sarcopenia diagnostic cutoff (CVD) and a percentile-rank exposure (cancer); neither carries the population SD needed to convert to a per-SD basis.

profile.mechanism: Usual gait speed is an integrative 'vital sign' that summarizes the function of the cardiovascular, musculoskeletal, neurological and energetic systems. It declines with age, predicts survival and disability across populations, and is one of the most validated single functional measures in geriatrics.

measurement.method: Time to walk a measured course (typically 4 m or 6 m) at usual pace, often the better of two trials; can also be measured over longer distances or with instrumented walkways.

profile.signal_quality_note: Highly reproducible and standardized; main variability is course length, static vs dynamic start and usual-pace instruction, all controllable.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 0.1 m/s higher usual gait speed
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 0.1; population SD 0.27 (Studenski 2011 JAMA pooled analysis: mean usual gait speed 0.92 m/s, SD 0.27 m/s (n=34,485 community-dwelling older adults).); k = SD/unit = 2.7

conversion.sd_source: Studenski 2011 JAMA pooled analysis: mean usual gait speed 0.92 m/s, SD 0.27 m/s (n=34,485 community-dwelling older adults).

all_cause_mortality.study: Studenski 2011, JAMA, pooled analysis of 9 cohort studies of community-dwelling older adults.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Studenski S, et al. Gait speed and survival in older adults. JAMA. 2011;305(1):50-58. doi:10.1001/jama.2010.1923. PMID:21205966
all_cause_mortality.notes: Pooled HR per 0.1 m/s higher gait speed 0.88 (0.87-0.90). The same paper provides the population SD (0.27 m/s) used for conversion. Follow-up ranged 6-21 years across 9 cohorts. Strong, graded, consistent association; gait speed predicted survival comparably to age and sex combined. Observational.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 0.1 m/s lower usual gait speed, incident cardiovascular disease
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 0.1; population SD 0.27 (Studenski 2011 JAMA pooled analysis: mean usual gait speed 0.92 m/s, SD 0.27 m/s (n=34,485 community-dwelling older adults).); k = SD/unit = 2.7

conversion.sd_source: Studenski 2011 JAMA pooled analysis: mean usual gait speed 0.92 m/s, SD 0.27 m/s (n=34,485 community-dwelling older adults).

cvd.study: Veronese 2018, JAMDA, systematic review/meta-analysis of 48 prospective cohorts (13 cohorts for the CVD outcome).
cvd.citation: Veronese N, et al. Association between gait speed with mortality, cardiovascular disease and cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies. J Am Med Dir Assoc. 2018;19(11):981-988.e7. doi:10.1016/j.jamda.2018.06.007. PMID:30056008
cvd.notes: Each 0.1 m/s slower gait speed: incident CVD HR 1.08 (1.03-1.13) across 13 cohorts (I2=81%). Same meta-analysis gives all-cause mortality HR 1.12 per 0.1 m/s. SD for conversion taken from Studenski 2011 (0.27 m/s), the same source used for the gait-speed mortality cell. Observational; partly reverse causation from subclinical CVD.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: per 0.1 m/s lower usual gait speed, cancer incidence/mortality
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 0.1; population SD 0.27 (Studenski 2011 JAMA pooled analysis: usual gait speed SD 0.27 m/s.); k = SD/unit = 2.7

conversion.sd_source: Studenski 2011 JAMA pooled analysis: usual gait speed SD 0.27 m/s.

cancer.study: Veronese 2018, JAMDA, meta-analysis of prospective cohorts.
cancer.citation: Veronese N, et al. J Am Med Dir Assoc. 2018;19(11):981-988.e7. doi:10.1016/j.jamda.2018.06.007. PMID:30056008
cancer.notes: Gait speed showed NO association with cancer (HR 1.00, 0.97-1.04 per 0.1 m/s; I2=15%, low heterogeneity). This is a genuine, well-powered null result, not missing data: unlike CVD and mortality, slow gait does not predict cancer. Retained as a real (null) cell so the heat map distinguishes 'tested, no effect' from 'no data'.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1-SD (0.204 m/s) lower usual gait speed, incident dementia
dementia.study: Three-City Study, prospective French population cohort: 1-SD lower gait speed associated with incident dementia.
dementia.citation: Dumurgier J, et al. Gait speed and decline in gait speed as predictors of incident dementia. J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. 2017;72(5):655-661. doi:10.1093/gerona/glw110. PMID:27302701. Per 1-SD (0.204 m/s) lower gait speed HR ~1.59.
dementia.notes: Three-City Study: per 1-SD (0.204 m/s) lower baseline gait speed, incident dementia HR ~1.59 (CI approximate). Replaces the earlier Beauchet 2016 categorical pooled estimate (poor vs good gait performance, RR ~1.53) with a native per-SD figure; the Beauchet meta-analysis remains consistent. UK Biobank wrist-sensor data show ~32% lower dementia hazard per SD faster maximal gait speed. Observational; preclinical dementia slows gait (reverse causation).

frailty.hr_unit_detail: slow gait speed as predictor of incident disability / frailty
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: slow gait speed is a defining Fried frailty phenotype criterion, partly definitional; no single clean independent HR
frailty.study: Slow gait speed is a core Fried frailty phenotype criterion and a strong predictor of incident mobility disability across cohorts.
frailty.citation: Fried LP, et al. Frailty in older adults: evidence for a phenotype. J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. 2001;56(3):M146-M156. PMID:11253156
frailty.notes: Slow gait speed is partly definitional for the frailty phenotype; it also strongly predicts incident disability. No single clean independent HR extracted here. Tier B given extensive consistent literature; effect size left null.

modifiability.intervention: Multicomponent exercise (aerobic + resistance + balance/gait training); resistance and power training in deconditioned older adults.
modifiability.effect_size: Exercise interventions improve gait speed by roughly 0.05-0.10+ m/s in older adults; ~0.05 m/s is a clinically meaningful change.
modifiability.citation: Exercise-intervention RCT meta-analyses in older adults show significant gait-speed improvement.

profile.mechanism: The chair-rise / sit-to-stand test measures lower-body strength, power and balance during a functionally essential task. Poor performance signals loss of mobility reserve, fall risk and frailty, and predicts mortality independent of conventional risk factors.

measurement.method: Time for five chair rises (5xSTS), number of rises in 30 seconds, or the floor-based sitting-rising test scored 0-10.

profile.signal_quality_note: Reproducible with standardized protocol; depends on chair height, arm use, technique and motivation, and different versions (timed, count, floor-based) are not interchangeable.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 1-point lower sitting-rising test score (0-10 scale), natural-cause mortality
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 2.2 (Araujo 2025 Eur J Prev Cardiol: sitting-rising test score mean 7.3 +/- 2.2 (n=4,282 adults aged 46-75).); k = SD/unit = 2.2

conversion.sd_source: Araujo 2025 Eur J Prev Cardiol: sitting-rising test score mean 7.3 +/- 2.2 (n=4,282 adults aged 46-75).

all_cause_mortality.study: Araujo 2025, Eur J Prev Cardiol, prospective cohort of adults 46-75 years performing the sitting-rising test.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Araujo CG, et al. Sitting-rising test scores predict natural and cardiovascular causes of deaths in middle-aged and older men and women. Eur J Prev Cardiol. 2025. doi:10.1093/eurjpc/zwaf325
all_cause_mortality.notes: Per-1-point continuous estimate now used instead of the prior lowest-vs-highest categorical contrast (HR 3.84): each 1-point lower SRT score raised natural-mortality risk by 33% (age-adjusted HR 1.33). The paper reports the SRT-score SD (2.2), enabling conversion to per-SD. The native per-point figure is age-adjusted (the multivariate model was reported only for the 5-group categorical contrast), so the CI is not available at the per-point level - flagged approximate. Observational; SRT is an ordinal 0-10 composite.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 1-point lower sitting-rising test score (0-10 scale), cardiovascular mortality
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 2.2 (Araujo 2025 Eur J Prev Cardiol: sitting-rising test score mean 7.3 +/- 2.2 (n=4,282 adults aged 46-75).); k = SD/unit = 2.2

conversion.sd_source: Araujo 2025 Eur J Prev Cardiol: sitting-rising test score mean 7.3 +/- 2.2 (n=4,282 adults aged 46-75).

cvd.study: Araujo 2025, Eur J Prev Cardiol, prospective cohort.
cvd.citation: Araujo CG, et al. Eur J Prev Cardiol. 2025. doi:10.1093/eurjpc/zwaf325
cvd.notes: Per-1-point continuous estimate replaces the prior lowest-vs-highest categorical contrast (HR 6.05): each 1-point lower SRT score raised cardiovascular-mortality risk by 31% (age-adjusted HR 1.31). SRT-score SD 2.2 enables per-SD conversion. Per-point figure is age-adjusted and the CI is not available at this level; single cohort with relatively few CV deaths - kept tier C and flagged approximate. Observational.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: slow 5x sit-to-stand time as predictor of incident frailty / disability
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: chair-rise performance is embedded in the SPPB and frailty phenotype, partly definitional; no clean standalone incident-frailty HR
frailty.study: Slow chair-rise time is a validated component of the Short Physical Performance Battery and predicts incident disability.
frailty.citation: Guralnik JM, et al. A short physical performance battery assessing lower extremity function. J Gerontol. 1994;49(2):M85-M94. PMID:8126356
frailty.notes: Chair-rise performance is embedded in the SPPB and frailty assessments; it predicts incident disability but a clean standalone incident-frailty HR is not extracted here. Effect size left null; tier C.

modifiability.intervention: Progressive resistance and power training targeting the lower body; practiced sit-to-stand exercise.
modifiability.effect_size: Chair-rise time and repetitions improve substantially with 8-12 weeks of resistance/power training in older adults.
modifiability.citation: Resistance- and power-training RCT meta-analyses in older adults.

profile.mechanism: VO2 kinetics describe how rapidly oxygen uptake rises at the onset of submaximal exercise (or recovers afterward). Faster kinetics indicate efficient oxygen delivery and mitochondrial oxidative responsiveness; sluggish kinetics reflect impaired microvascular and mitochondrial function and predict poorer functional mobility in older and chronically ill adults.

measurement.method: Breath-by-breath gas analysis during constant-load submaximal exercise transitions or recovery, fitted to exponential models to derive mean response time / tau.

profile.signal_quality_note: Breath-by-breath data are inherently noisy; reliable kinetic parameter estimation requires multiple transitions and signal averaging, and modeling choices affect results. Not standardized for routine use.
profile.testing_cadence: not routinely tracked; research/clinical-physiology use

frailty.hr_unit_detail: slower VO2 kinetics associated with poorer functional mobility in impaired older adults
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: cross-sectional association only; no hard-outcome HR or usable SD published, cannot be standardized
frailty.study: Cross-sectional and small cohort studies link slow submaximal VO2 on- and recovery-kinetics to functional mobility limitation and lower muscle strength in older adults.
frailty.citation: Bell C, et al. Oxygen-uptake (VO2) kinetics and functional mobility performance in impaired older adults. J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. 2003;58(1):M36-M40. PMID:12560409
frailty.notes: Evidence is mechanistic/cross-sectional - slow VO2 kinetics relate to mobility limitation and muscle function in older and chronic-disease populations - but re-search confirmed no hard-outcome HR exists. Left unconvertible, tier C. Largely an intermediate physiologic marker.

modifiability.intervention: Endurance / aerobic training; high-intensity interval training.
modifiability.effect_size: VO2 on-kinetics speed up (shorter tau / mean response time) with aerobic training, particularly in older and deconditioned individuals.
modifiability.citation: Exercise-training studies show acceleration of VO2 kinetics with endurance training.

profile.mechanism: Heart rate recovery is the fall in heart rate in the first 1-2 minutes after peak exercise, driven mainly by parasympathetic (vagal) reactivation. Attenuated recovery indicates autonomic dysfunction, an independent marker of cardiovascular risk and mortality.

measurement.method: Difference between peak heart rate and heart rate at 1 (or 2) minutes after a graded exercise test, with a standardized cool-down.

profile.signal_quality_note: Reproducible when cool-down protocol (active vs passive recovery, posture) is standardized; protocol differences affect absolute values and abnormality cutoffs vary across studies.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual (with exercise testing)

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: per 10-bpm lower 1-minute heart rate recovery, all-cause mortality
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 10; population SD 12 (1-minute HRR population SD ~12 bpm, from primary-prevention treadmill-test cohorts (e.g. Cole 1999 NEJM-type distributions; median ~17, IQR-derived SD ~12 bpm).); k = SD/unit = 1.2

conversion.sd_source: 1-minute HRR population SD ~12 bpm, from primary-prevention treadmill-test cohorts (e.g. Cole 1999 NEJM-type distributions; median ~17, IQR-derived SD ~12 bpm).

all_cause_mortality.study: Qiu 2017, JAHA, meta-analysis of 9 prospective cohort studies.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Qiu S, et al. Heart rate recovery and risk of cardiovascular events and all-cause mortality: a meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies. J Am Heart Assoc. 2017;6(5):e005505. doi:10.1161/JAHA.117.005505. PMID:28487388
all_cause_mortality.notes: Per 10-bpm lower HRR: all-cause mortality HR 1.09 (1.01-1.19). The continuous per-10-bpm estimate (rather than the attenuated-vs-fast categorical contrast HR 1.68, 1.51-1.88) is used so the cell can be standardized to per-SD. 9 studies, 2,082 deaths among 41,600 participants. Association independent of metabolic risk factors. Observational.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: per 10-bpm lower 1-minute heart rate recovery, cardiovascular events
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 10; population SD 12 (1-minute HRR population SD ~12 bpm, primary-prevention treadmill-test cohorts.); k = SD/unit = 1.2

conversion.sd_source: 1-minute HRR population SD ~12 bpm, primary-prevention treadmill-test cohorts.

cvd.study: Qiu 2017, JAHA, meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies.
cvd.notes: Per 10-bpm lower HRR: cardiovascular events HR 1.13 (1.05-1.21). The continuous per-10-bpm estimate is used (categorical attenuated-vs-fast HR 1.69, 1.05-2.71) so the cell standardizes to per-SD. 5 studies, 1,061 events among 34,267 participants. Observational.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: per 1-SD higher heart-rate-recovery index after exercise, incident all-cause dementia
dementia.study: UK Biobank prospective cohort; heart-rate response and recovery during a submaximal cycle exercise test and incident dementia.
dementia.citation: Heart rate response and recovery during exercise and dementia risk: a prospective UK Biobank study. Sci Rep. 2025;15. doi:10.1038/s41598-025-22299-2. PMID:41184419
dementia.notes: UK Biobank (n=46,348, median 12.6 y, 519 dementia cases): per 1-SD higher HRR index, incident all-cause dementia HR 0.90 (0.82-0.99) in the age/sex-adjusted model; with full risk-factor adjustment the per-SD HR was 0.72 (0.54-0.94). The recovery-ratio metric gave a concordant per-SD HR 0.90 (0.81-0.99). Native per-SD estimate; the less-adjusted Model 1 figure is used as the conservative comparable value. Associations with Alzheimer's and vascular dementia specifically were not significant. Observational.

modifiability.intervention: Aerobic / endurance exercise training, which enhances vagal tone.
modifiability.effect_size: Heart rate recovery improves by several bpm with regular aerobic training; trained individuals show faster recovery.
modifiability.citation: Exercise-training trials demonstrate improved heart rate recovery with aerobic training.

profile.mechanism: Heart rate variability quantifies beat-to-beat variation in heart rate, indexing autonomic (mainly parasympathetic) regulation. Higher HRV reflects healthy vagal tone and physiologic adaptability; HRV declines with age and disease and lower values predict mortality and cardiovascular events.

measurement.method: Time-domain (SDNN, RMSSD) and frequency-domain measures from ECG or photoplethysmography over short recordings (~5 min), 24-hour Holter, or consumer wearables.

profile.signal_quality_note: HRV is highly state-dependent (posture, breathing, stress, sleep, caffeine, time of day) with large day-to-day variability; recording length and device differ across studies. Trends are more informative than single values.
confounders: physical activity and stress before recording
profile.testing_cadence: trend monitoring (e.g., daily wearable) or baseline + annual clinical recording

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: lowest vs other quartiles of 5-minute RMSSD, all-cause mortality (healthy and patient populations)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
all_cause_mortality.study: Jarczok 2022, Neurosci Biobehav Rev, systematic review/meta-analysis of HRV and mortality across healthy and patient populations (32 studies, 2 datasets).
all_cause_mortality.citation: Jarczok MN, et al. Heart rate variability in the prediction of mortality: a systematic review and meta-analysis of healthy and patient populations. Neurosci Biobehav Rev. 2022;143:104907. doi:10.1016/j.neubiorev.2022.104907. PMID:36243195
all_cause_mortality.notes: Pooled HR 1.56 (1.32-1.85) for the lowest 5-minute RMSSD quartile vs other quartiles, across 38,008 participants in healthy and clinical populations. Converted to per-SD using an extreme-quartile approximation (HRV indices are log-normally distributed and analyzed log-transformed). Replaces the prior CVD-patient-only estimate (Fang 2020, low vs high HRV HR 2.12, 1.64-2.75) with a broader general-and-patient-population figure. Observational.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: lowest vs highest level of SDNN, first cardiovascular event in populations without known CVD
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: median_split contrast; k = 1/1.6 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cvd.study: Hillebrand 2013, Europace, meta-analysis and dose-response meta-regression in populations without known CVD.
cvd.citation: Hillebrand S, et al. Heart rate variability and first cardiovascular event in populations without known cardiovascular disease: meta-analysis and dose-response meta-regression. Europace. 2013;15(5):742-749. doi:10.1093/europace/eus341. PMID:23425965
cvd.notes: Pooled RR 1.35 (1.10-1.67) comparing lowest vs highest SDNN level for a first cardiovascular event in populations without known CVD (the commonly cited '~40% higher risk'). Conservatively converted with an above/below-median separation; the original studies' grouping was heterogeneous (medians/tertiles), so this is an approximate conversion. Observational.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: lower HRV associated with shorter survival in cancer patients; no general-population cancer-incidence HR
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: evidence is prognostic (HRV measured in established-cancer patients predicting survival), not an incidence hazard in a cancer-free population; the two are not comparable, so no per-SD incidence HR can be assigned
cancer.study: Systematic reviews/meta-analyses of HRV as a prognostic factor for survival in patients already diagnosed with cancer.
cancer.citation: Zhou X, et al. Heart rate variability in the prediction of survival in patients with cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Psychosom Res. 2016;89:20-25. PMID:27663106. Higher SDNN protective for overall survival, HR ~0.59 (0.46-0.75).
cancer.notes: HRV evidence in cancer is PROGNOSTIC - higher SDNN predicts longer survival in already-diagnosed cancer patients (HR ~0.59) - not predictive of cancer INCIDENCE in a cancer-free population. These are not comparable to the incidence-based cells used elsewhere in the heat map. No general-population HRV-to-cancer-incidence per-SD HR exists. Left unconvertible/qualitative; tier C. Reverse causation is severe (tumour burden lowers HRV).

dementia.hr_unit_detail: lower vagally-mediated HRV associated with faster cognitive decline; no incident-dementia per-SD HR
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: available longitudinal evidence quantifies accelerated cognitive decline (effect on a continuous cognitive score), not incident-dementia hazard; no per-SD HR for incident dementia could be sourced
dementia.study: Whitehall II cohort: midlife vagally-mediated HRV (RMSSD, HF-HRV) and 10-year cognitive decline; plus narrative reviews of HRV and cognition.
dementia.citation: Britton A / Whitehall II analyses: Midlife heart rate variability and cognitive decline: a large longitudinal cohort study. 2024. PMID:39639946. Low RMSSD and HF-HRV were associated with cognitive decline ~3-3.5 years faster per decade.
dementia.notes: Lower midlife vagally-mediated HRV predicts faster cognitive decline (low RMSSD: 0.07 SD faster 10-year decline), but the outcome is rate of cognitive decline, not an incident-dementia hazard. No verified per-SD HR for incident dementia was located. Left unconvertible/qualitative rather than borrowing a cognitive-decline effect onto a hazard scale. Direction (lower HRV -> worse) is consistent. Tier C.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: lower HRV associated with frailty; cross-sectional/odds-ratio evidence, no incident-frailty per-SD HR
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: available evidence is cross-sectional (lower HRV in frail vs non-frail; odds ratios) with a 2025 meta-analysis finding non-significant pooled differences; no prospective per-SD incident-frailty hazard ratio could be sourced
frailty.study: Cross-sectional studies and a 2025 three-level meta-analysis of HRV and frailty; lower time- and frequency-domain HRV in (pre)frail older adults, but pooled effects were not statistically significant.
frailty.citation: Parvaneh S, et al. Physiological complexity underlying heart rate dynamics and frailty status in community-dwelling older women. Gerontology 2010; and 2025 systematic review/three-level meta-analysis of HRV and frailty (non-significant pooled difference).
frailty.notes: Lower HRV is associated with frailty cross-sectionally (e.g. lower heart-rate approximate entropy: OR ~1.99 for being frail), but a 2025 meta-analysis found pooled HRV differences between frail and non-frail groups non-significant, and no prospective per-SD incident-frailty hazard ratio exists. Left unconvertible/qualitative; tier C. Largely a correlate rather than a validated prospective predictor.

modifiability.intervention: Aerobic exercise training; secondarily slow-paced breathing / HRV-biofeedback and improved sleep and stress management.
modifiability.effect_size: Aerobic training produces modest increases in HRV (RMSSD/SDNN); biofeedback can acutely raise HRV but durable resting changes are smaller.
modifiability.citation: Meta-analyses of exercise training and of HRV-biofeedback show modest HRV improvements.

profile.mechanism: Acute-phase protein synthesized by the liver in response to IL-6; serves as a downstream integrator of systemic low-grade inflammation ('inflammaging'). Elevated hsCRP marks the chronic inflammatory state that accompanies atherosclerosis, metabolic dysfunction, and biological aging, though Mendelian randomization indicates it is a marker rather than a cause of disease.

measurement.method: High-sensitivity immunoturbidimetric or immunonephelometric assay on a standard clinical chemistry analyzer.

profile.signal_quality_note: Analytically robust and well-standardized, but high within-person biological variability: levels spike acutely with infection, trauma, or recent exercise. Single measurements are noisy; serial measurements or values >10 mg/L should be excluded as acute reactions. Tertile/quartile stability over years is moderate.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual; repeat if >10 mg/L to exclude acute inflammation

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher log-e CRP, non-vascular (non-cardiovascular) mortality, adjusted for conventional risk factors
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
all_cause_mortality.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration 2010, Lancet, individual-participant meta-analysis of 54 prospective studies.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration. C-reactive protein concentration and risk of coronary heart disease, stroke, and mortality: an individual participant meta-analysis. Lancet. 2010;375(9709):132-140. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(09)61717-7. PMID: 20031199.
all_cause_mortality.notes: REFINED: replaces the earlier categorical Li 2017 pooled estimate with the ERFC native per-SD-of-log-CRP figure. ERFC reports non-vascular mortality RR 1.55 (1.41-1.69) age/sex-adjusted, 1.54 (1.40-1.68) after full risk-factor adjustment; vascular-mortality and all-cause associations are of broadly similar size. Strong predictive association but NOT causal -- the CRP CHD Genetics Collaboration MR found CRP-raising variants unrelated to disease.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher log-e CRP, adjusted for conventional risk factors (coronary heart disease)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
cvd.study: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration 2010, Lancet, individual-participant meta-analysis of 54 prospective studies.
cvd.citation: Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration. C-reactive protein concentration and risk of coronary heart disease, stroke, and mortality: an individual participant meta-analysis. Lancet. 2010;375(9709):132-140. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(09)61717-7. PMID: 20031199.
cvd.notes: HR is 1.63 (1.51-1.76) with age/sex adjustment only, attenuating to 1.37 after full risk-factor adjustment, indicating substantial confounding. CRITICAL: the CRP CHD Genetics Collaboration Mendelian randomization study (BMJ 2011;342:d548, ~194,000 participants) found CRP-raising genetic variants were NOT associated with coronary disease, concluding CRP concentration is unlikely to be even a modest causal factor. hsCRP is a strong RISK PREDICTOR but not a causal target -- this is where the field oversells.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 natural-log unit higher CRP, overall cancer incidence (pooled prospective cohorts)
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 1.03 (SD of natural-log CRP ~1.0-1.1 in general adult populations (NHANES / ERFC-type distributions: median ~2 mg/L, IQR ~1-4.5 mg/L); ~1.03 used as a central estimate. Because the SD of ln(CRP) is close to 1, the per-log-unit and per-log-SD figures nearly coincide.); k = SD/unit = 1.03

conversion.sd_source: SD of natural-log CRP ~1.0-1.1 in general adult populations (NHANES / ERFC-type distributions: median ~2 mg/L, IQR ~1-4.5 mg/L); ~1.03 used as a central estimate. Because the SD of ln(CRP) is close to 1, the per-log-unit and per-log-SD figures nearly coincide.

cancer.study: Guo 2013, Asian Pacific J Cancer Prev, meta-analysis of 11 prospective cohort studies (11,459 cancer cases).
cancer.citation: Guo YZ, Pan L, Du CJ, Ren DQ, Xie XM. Association between C-reactive protein and risk of cancer: a meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies. Asian Pac J Cancer Prev. 2013;14(1):243-248. doi:10.7314/apjcp.2013.14.1.243. PMID: 23534731.
cancer.notes: REFINED: replaces the earlier categorical Li 2017 cancer-mortality pooled estimate with a continuous per-ln-unit HR for cancer INCIDENCE (1.105, 1.033-1.178). Signal is driven by lung cancer (HR 1.31 per ln-unit); breast/prostate/colorectal associations were not significant. Converted via the SD of ln(CRP); since that SD is ~1, the per-log-SD figure is nearly identical to the native per-ln-unit HR. Mendelian randomization does NOT support a causal CRP-cancer link -- the association likely reflects reverse causation (preclinical tumor inflammation) and confounding (smoking). Predictive, not causal.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher CRP, all-cause dementia
dementia.study: Darweesh 2018, Alzheimer's & Dementia, meta-analysis of inflammatory markers and dementia risk.
dementia.citation: Darweesh SKL et al. Inflammatory markers and the risk of dementia and Alzheimer's disease: a meta-analysis. Alzheimers Dement. 2018;14(11):1450-1459. doi:10.1016/j.jalz.2018.02.014.
dementia.notes: CRP associated with all-cause dementia but NOT specifically with Alzheimer's disease in the same meta-analysis; signal appears driven by vascular dementia. Reverse causation (prodromal inflammation) is a concern given the long preclinical dementia phase.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher CRP, incident frailty in women over 4 years (fully adjusted odds ratio)
frailty.study: Gale 2013, English Longitudinal Study of Ageing, prospective cohort (women subset; 150 incident frailty cases).
frailty.citation: Gale CR et al. Inflammatory markers and incident frailty in men and women: the English Longitudinal Study of Ageing. Age (Dordr). 2013;35(6):2493-2501. doi:10.1007/s11357-013-9528-9. PMID: 23543263.
frailty.notes: REFINED: replaces the earlier Soysal log-unit estimate with a native per-SD figure. Fully-adjusted OR 1.27 (0.96-1.69) in women (borderline; age-adjusted OR was 1.69, 1.32-2.17); NO association in men (OR 0.93, 0.66-1.30). Soysal 2016 meta-analysis of 3 longitudinal studies likewise found CRP did not significantly predict incident frailty. Honest read: CRP tracks WITH frailty more than it forecasts it, and the prospective signal is sex-specific and weak.

modifiability.intervention: Statin therapy, weight loss, and regular aerobic exercise; canakinumab/colchicine for residual inflammatory risk.
modifiability.effect_size: Rosuvastatin lowered hsCRP ~37% in JUPITER; diet-induced weight loss produces a comparable reduction (roughly proportional to kg lost); regular exercise lowers hsCRP by ~20-30%.
modifiability.timeframe: Weeks for statins; 3-6 months for weight loss and exercise.
modifiability.citation: Ridker PM et al. JUPITER trial. N Engl J Med. 2008;359:2195-2207. POUNDS LOST trial (weight loss & hsCRP), Am J Clin Nutr 2012.

profile.mechanism: Pleiotropic pro-inflammatory cytokine and the principal driver of hepatic acute-phase protein synthesis (including CRP and fibrinogen). A central mediator of 'inflammaging'; unlike CRP, human genetic (IL6R) evidence supports a causal role in atherosclerotic disease, making the IL-6 pathway a validated therapeutic target.

measurement.method: ELISA or high-sensitivity immunoassay (e.g., electrochemiluminescence); proteomic panels (Olink, SomaScan) also quantify it.

profile.signal_quality_note: Biologically meaningful but analytically more variable than CRP: short half-life, diurnal variation, sensitivity to recent exercise/infection, and assay-platform differences. Single measurements are noisy; cross-assay comparability is limited.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + annual; not yet routine in standard care

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher log IL-6, all-cause mortality (adjusted)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
all_cause_mortality.study: Lee S et al. 2012, Rancho Bernardo Study, general-population older-adult prospective cohort; concordant with Framingham immune-phenotype analysis.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Lee S et al. Serum interleukin-6 concentrations and mortality in older adults: the Rancho Bernardo Study. PLoS One. 2012;7(4):e34218. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0034218. PMID: 22509280. Corroborated by Framingham Heart Study immune-phenotype analysis 2024, PMID: 38867269.
all_cause_mortality.notes: Multiple large cohorts (Rancho Bernardo HR ~1.48 per SD log IL-6; Framingham) converge on HR ~1.4-1.5 per SD; association persists after CRP adjustment, indicating IL-6 carries independent prognostic information. Tier A from consistency across cohorts. Native per-SD-of-log estimate -- IL-6 is right-skewed and analyzed log-transformed.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher log IL-6, non-fatal MI / coronary heart disease (risk-factor adjusted; pooled cohort estimate)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
cvd.study: IL6R Mendelian Randomisation Analysis Consortium 2012, Lancet (causal); observational per-SD HR from Emerging Risk Factors Collaboration-type pooled cohort data.
cvd.citation: IL6R Genetics Consortium / Sarwar N et al. The interleukin-6 receptor as a target for prevention of coronary heart disease: a mendelian randomisation analysis. Lancet. 2012;379(9822):1205-1213. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(11)60110-X. PMID: 22421340.
cvd.notes: IMPORTANT contrast with CRP: the IL6R variant (rs7529229, marking p.Asp358Ala) that mimics IL-6 signaling blockade was associated with LOWER coronary heart disease (per-allele OR 0.95, 0.93-0.97) in ~25,458 cases / 100,740 controls -- genetic evidence DOES support a causal role for IL-6 signaling. Observational per-SD HR is approximate (pooled cohort estimates ~1.2-1.3); causal validation is the key point. This anchors the canakinumab (CANTOS) and colchicine cardiovascular trials.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher log IL-6, cancer mortality (approximate, older-adult cohort)
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR per 1 SD of log-transformed marker
cancer.study: Rancho Bernardo Study and similar elderly cohorts reporting IL-6 with cancer mortality.
cancer.citation: Lee S et al. Serum interleukin-6 concentrations and mortality in older adults: the Rancho Bernardo Study. PLoS One. 2012;7(4):e34218. PMID: 22509280.
cancer.notes: IL-6 elevation associates with cancer mortality in observational cohorts, but no pooled meta-analytic estimate confirmed and reverse causation (tumor-secreted IL-6) is a major concern. Estimate is an order-of-magnitude approximation; treat as low-confidence.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher IL-6, all-cause dementia
dementia.study: Darweesh 2018, Alzheimer's & Dementia, meta-analysis of inflammatory markers and dementia risk.
dementia.citation: Darweesh SKL et al. Inflammatory markers and the risk of dementia and Alzheimer's disease: a meta-analysis. Alzheimers Dement. 2018;14(11):1450-1459. doi:10.1016/j.jalz.2018.02.014.
dementia.notes: REFINED to the verified Darweesh pooled figure: IL-6 HR 1.32 (1.06-1.64) for all-cause dementia, but NOT associated with Alzheimer's disease alone (HR 1.06, 0.83-1.35) -- signal appears vascular. Reverse causation across the long dementia prodrome remains a caveat.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 unit higher natural-log IL-6, incident frailty (longitudinal subset; OR reported as risk ratio)
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 0.75 (SD of natural-log IL-6 ~0.75 in older-adult populations, derived from a log-normal fit to a representative older-adult distribution (linear mean ~2.0 pg/mL, SD ~1.74 pg/mL; CV ~0.87 implies SD of ln(IL-6) = sqrt(ln(1+CV^2)) ~0.75). Consistent with the 0.6-0.8 range seen across elderly cohorts. Approximate.); k = SD/unit = 0.75

conversion.sd_source: SD of natural-log IL-6 ~0.75 in older-adult populations, derived from a log-normal fit to a representative older-adult distribution (linear mean ~2.0 pg/mL, SD ~1.74 pg/mL; CV ~0.87 implies SD of ln(IL-6) = sqrt(ln(1+CV^2)) ~0.75). Consistent with the 0.6-0.8 range seen across elderly cohorts. Approximate.

frailty.study: Soysal 2016, Ageing Research Reviews, meta-analysis (3 longitudinal studies for incident frailty).
frailty.citation: Soysal P et al. Inflammation and frailty in the elderly: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ageing Res Rev. 2016;31:1-8. doi:10.1016/j.arr.2016.08.006. PMID: 27592340.
frailty.notes: REFINED: previously unconvertible. The native HR is per 1 unit of ln(IL-6) with no SD reported in the source; converted using a population SD of ln(IL-6) ~0.75 derived from a log-normal fit to older-adult IL-6 distributions -- an approximate, externally-sourced SD, so verification_status is downgraded to approximate. Strong cross-sectional IL-6-frailty link, but the 3 longitudinal studies did NOT show IL-6 significantly predicting incident frailty (CI crosses 1). Related pooled analyses show IL-6 predicting incident mobility disability (HR ~1.26 per log unit).

modifiability.intervention: Direct pathway blockade (IL-6R antibody tocilizumab; IL-1beta antibody canakinumab; colchicine) lowers IL-6 axis activity; lifestyle (exercise, weight loss) produces smaller reductions.
modifiability.effect_size: Canakinumab (CANTOS) reduced hsCRP ~37% and IL-6 substantially with a 15% cut in major cardiovascular events; tocilizumab strongly suppresses CRP/fibrinogen. Exercise and weight loss lower IL-6 modestly (~10-25%).
modifiability.timeframe: Days to weeks for biologics; months for lifestyle change.
modifiability.citation: Ridker PM et al. CANTOS trial (canakinumab). N Engl J Med. 2017;377:1119-1131. IL6R MR consortium, Lancet 2012.

profile.mechanism: Pro-inflammatory cytokine central to innate immunity, apoptosis signaling, insulin resistance, and cachexia. Implicated in inflammaging and tissue catabolism; its soluble receptors (sTNFR1/sTNFR2) are more stable, prognostically informative analytes than free TNF-alpha itself.

measurement.method: High-sensitivity ELISA or multiplex immunoassay; soluble TNF receptors (sTNFR1/2) often preferred for stability.

profile.signal_quality_note: Free circulating TNF-alpha is present at very low concentrations with a short half-life, marked diurnal variation, and poor assay reproducibility near the detection limit. Soluble receptors are far more reliable; most robust prognostic data come from sTNFR measurements rather than TNF-alpha itself.
confounders: assay sensitivity limits and platform differences
profile.testing_cadence: research / specialist use; not routine

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD (2.81 pg/mL) higher TNF-alpha, all-cause mortality in very old adults
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
all_cause_mortality.study: Bruunsgaard 2003, American Journal of Medicine, prospective study of TNF-alpha and mortality in centenarians.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Bruunsgaard H et al. Elevated levels of tumor necrosis factor alpha and mortality in centenarians. Am J Med. 2003;115(4):278-283. doi:10.1016/S0002-9343(03)00329-2. PMID: 12967692.
all_cause_mortality.notes: REFINED: previously a null/qualitative cell. A genuine native per-SD HR exists, but only in a centenarian cohort -- a highly selected oldest-old population, so generalizability to general-population mortality is limited (hence Tier C). Free TNF-alpha is also analytically noisy near the detection limit. Soluble TNF receptors (sTNFR1) are the more robust analyte and consistently predict mortality (e.g., NOMAS Q4-vs-Q1 HR 1.8), but that is a distinct biomarker.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: sTNFR1 tertile 3 vs tertile 1, death or new heart failure after acute MI (soluble TNF receptor, not free TNF-alpha)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: tertile contrast; k = 1/2.18 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cvd.study: Valgimigli 2005, Circulation, soluble TNF receptor-1 after acute myocardial infarction.
cvd.citation: Valgimigli M et al. Tumor necrosis factor-alpha receptor 1 is a major predictor of mortality and new-onset heart failure in patients with acute myocardial infarction. Circulation. 2005;111(7):863-870. PMID: 15699251.
cvd.notes: Estimate is for the soluble TNF receptor (sTNFR1) in a post-MI population, NOT free TNF-alpha in primary prevention. Although a tertile contrast, it is flagged categorical (not converted) because the analyte/population mismatch makes a per-SD placement misleading. Strong prognostic marker in established CVD. Tier C reflects the mismatch.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher soluble TNF receptor 1 (sTNFR1), cancer mortality (soluble TNF receptor, not free TNF-alpha)
cancer.study: Carlsson 2014, Atherosclerosis, ULSAM community cohort of elderly men.
cancer.citation: Carlsson AC, Juhlin CC, Larsson TE, et al. Soluble tumor necrosis factor receptor 1 (sTNFR1) is associated with increased total mortality due to cancer and cardiovascular causes -- findings from two community based cohorts of elderly. Atherosclerosis. 2014;237(1):236-242. doi:10.1016/j.atherosclerosis.2014.09.005. PMID: 25255422.
cancer.notes: REFINED: previously a null cell. A genuine native per-SD HR exists (1.32, 1.11-1.57) but for the soluble receptor sTNFR1, NOT free TNF-alpha -- consistent with how the TNF-alpha/cvd cell handles the same analyte mismatch. sTNFR1 is the stable, prognostically informative analyte; free TNF-alpha is analytically noisy. Because of the analyte mismatch the figure is flagged out of the comparable per-SD scale rather than placed on it. Concordant evidence: circulating sTNFR2 associates with colorectal cancer risk (meta-analytic OR ~1.59). Single elderly-men cohort -- Tier C.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: Top vs bottom tertile of PBMC spontaneous TNF-alpha production, incident Alzheimer's disease (PBMC production, not plasma concentration)
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: tertile contrast; k = 1/2.18 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
dementia.study: Tan 2007, Neurology, Framingham Study, cognitively intact community-dwelling older adults (mean age 79).
dementia.citation: Tan ZS, Beiser AS, Vasan RS, et al. Inflammatory markers and the risk of Alzheimer disease: the Framingham Study. Neurology. 2007;68(22):1902-1908. doi:10.1212/01.wnl.0000263217.36439.da. PMID: 17536046.
dementia.notes: REFINED: previously a null cell. Framingham reported TNF-alpha top-tertile HR 2.59 (1.09-6.12) for incident Alzheimer's disease. IMPORTANT analyte caveat: TNF-alpha was measured as SPONTANEOUS PRODUCTION by peripheral blood mononuclear cells, not circulating plasma concentration -- a related but distinct exposure (free plasma TNF-alpha is analytically noisy near the detection limit). Converted from a tertile contrast. Wide CI, single cohort, distinct analyte -- Tier C. The broader inflammatory-marker meta-analyses (Darweesh 2018) did not isolate a TNF-alpha plasma figure for dementia.

frailty.study: Soysal 2016 meta-analysis reports cross-sectional TNF-alpha elevation in frail individuals; no significant longitudinal prediction.
frailty.citation: Soysal P et al. Inflammation and frailty in the elderly. Ageing Res Rev. 2016;31:1-8. PMID: 27592340.
frailty.notes: Searched specifically for an incident-frailty HR: TNF-alpha is cross-sectionally higher in frail vs robust older adults, but no verified longitudinal HR for incident frailty exists -- the inflammatory-marker meta-analyses found inflammatory parameters generally do not predict frailty onset, and free TNF-alpha is analytically noisy. Left without a numeric cell to avoid fabricated precision; honest emptiness.

modifiability.intervention: Anti-TNF biologics (etanercept, infliximab, adalimumab) in inflammatory disease; lifestyle (weight loss, exercise) for modest reductions in the general population.
modifiability.effect_size: Anti-TNF therapy strongly suppresses TNF signaling and, in observational meta-analysis of rheumatoid arthritis cohorts, was associated with reduced cardiovascular events (RR ~0.46); lifestyle change yields modest reductions.
modifiability.timeframe: Days to weeks for biologics; months for lifestyle.
modifiability.citation: Barnabe C et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis: anti-TNF therapy and cardiovascular events in rheumatoid arthritis. Arthritis Care Res. 2011;63(4):522-529. PMID: 20957658.

profile.mechanism: Composite NMR signal arising from N-acetyl methyl groups of glycosylated acute-phase proteins (alpha-1-acid glycoprotein, haptoglobin, alpha-1-antitrypsin, alpha-1-antichymotrypsin, transferrin). Integrates both protein abundance and glycosylation state, capturing a stable, time-averaged measure of chronic systemic inflammation that predicts long-term disease risk.

measurement.method: Proton (1H) nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy of plasma/serum, typically as part of a multi-analyte NMR metabolomics panel.

profile.signal_quality_note: Lower within-person biological variability than CRP because it averages several glycoproteins with longer half-lives; NMR quantification is highly reproducible. Less sensitive to transient acute spikes, making it a comparatively stable inflammation index. Newer marker with less standardization across labs.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + every few years; not yet routine clinical use

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Top vs bottom quartile of GlycA, pooled all-cause mortality across 7 cohorts
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
all_cause_mortality.study: Connelly 2019, J Intern Med, meta-analysis of 7 population-based cohorts including the PREVEND study.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Connelly MA et al. GlycA, a novel pro-inflammatory glycoprotein biomarker, is associated with mortality: results from the PREVEND study and meta-analysis. J Intern Med. 2019;286(5):596-609. doi:10.1111/joim.12953. PMID: 31260573.
all_cause_mortality.notes: Pooled RR 1.74 top vs bottom quartile across 7 cohorts (8,153 deaths). PREVEND primary HR 1.43 (1.09-1.87). Consistent with Women's Health Study per-SD HR ~1.14. Newer marker -- evidence base shallower than CRP/IL-6; Tier B. Converted from quartile contrast as no pooled native per-SD estimate is available.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: Quartile 4 vs quartile 1 of GlycA, incident cardiovascular disease
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cvd.study: Akinkuolie 2014, Women's Health Study, prospective cohort of initially healthy women.
cvd.citation: Akinkuolie AO et al. A novel protein glycan biomarker and future cardiovascular disease events. J Am Heart Assoc. 2014;3(5):e001221. doi:10.1161/JAHA.114.001221. PMID: 25249300.
cvd.notes: Graded quartile relationship. Note: the PREVEND meta-analysis found GlycA was NOT independently associated with CARDIOVASCULAR mortality after full adjustment (nor was hsCRP) -- the incident-CVD signal partly attenuates with adjustment. No Mendelian randomization evidence; causality unestablished. Field should not yet treat GlycA as a causal target.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher GlycA, cancer mortality at maximal follow-up
cancer.study: Duprez / Otvos 2016, Women's Health Study, circulating N-linked glycoprotein acetyls and longitudinal mortality.
cancer.citation: Duprez DA et al. Circulating N-linked glycoprotein acetyls and longitudinal mortality risk. Circ Res. 2016;118(7):1146-1153. doi:10.1161/CIRCRESAHA.115.308078. PMID: 26951635.
cancer.notes: Modest association; stronger at 5 years (HR 1.23, 1.02-1.47) than at maximal follow-up, suggesting partial reverse causation from preclinical malignancy. A separate Women's Health Study analysis found GlycA predicts colorectal cancer incidence (HR 1.19 per SD, 1.06-1.35). Single-cohort, single-sex data -- Tier C.

dementia.study: Harshfield 2023, Neurology, UK Biobank metabolomic profiles and incident stroke/dementia.
dementia.citation: Harshfield EL, Markus HS. Association of baseline metabolomic profiles with incident stroke and dementia and with imaging markers of cerebral small vessel disease. Neurology. 2023;101(5):e489-e501. doi:10.1212/WNL.0000000000207242.
dementia.notes: Searched specifically: the largest, best-controlled prospective analysis (UK Biobank, 118,021 participants, median 13.1y) found GlycA was NOT significantly associated with incident ALL-CAUSE dementia after adjustment for vascular risk factors. GlycA does predict incident STROKE and is reported to associate with vascular dementia, but no convertible, adjusted all-cause-dementia HR with CI was found. Left null rather than misrepresent a stroke/vascular-dementia signal as an all-cause-dementia HR -- honest emptiness.

frailty.study: Multicohort observational and Mendelian randomization analyses link GlycA to the Frailty Index.
frailty.citation: Liu D et al. Unraveling the metabolic underpinnings of frailty using multicohort observational and Mendelian randomization analyses. Aging Cell. 2023. PMC10410014.
frailty.notes: GlycA had the strongest positive association with the Frailty Index among NMR biomarkers (+1.40% Frailty Index per SD, 1.35-1.44), and MR supported a causal effect (beta +0.37% per SD). However, these are continuous Frailty-Index slope estimates, NOT a hazard ratio for incident frailty -- no convertible time-to-event HR was found, so the cell is left without a numeric HR rather than misrepresenting a slope as an HR.

modifiability.intervention: Weight loss, exercise, and statin therapy lower GlycA, paralleling effects on other acute-phase markers.
modifiability.effect_size: Statins and lifestyle interventions reduce GlycA modestly (roughly 5-15%); magnitude tracks reductions in adiposity and CRP.
modifiability.citation: Observational and secondary-analysis data (e.g., statin trials with NMR substudies); no dedicated large RCT with GlycA as primary endpoint.

profile.mechanism: Ratio derived from a standard complete blood count; integrates the innate (neutrophil) and adaptive (lymphocyte) immune compartments. A high ratio reflects active inflammatory/stress responses with relative lymphopenia, a pattern associated with immunosenescence, physiologic stress, and adverse aging trajectories.

measurement.method: Calculated from an automated complete blood count with differential (absolute neutrophil count divided by absolute lymphocyte count).

profile.signal_quality_note: Cheap and ubiquitous (derived from routine CBC), but highly non-specific and labile: shifts acutely with infection, physical/psychological stress, corticosteroids, and time of day. No standardized cut-points; tertile/quartile definitions vary widely across studies, limiting comparability.
profile.testing_cadence: opportunistic -- available on any CBC; interpret with clinical context

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Per one-quartile increase in NLR, all-cause mortality (US general population)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
all_cause_mortality.study: NHANES 1999-2014 general-population analysis of NLR and mortality.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Song M et al. Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio and mortality in the United States general population. Sci Rep. 2021;11:464. doi:10.1038/s41598-020-79431-7.
all_cause_mortality.notes: REFINED: replaces the loosely-defined Rotterdam per-unit estimate with the NHANES per-quartile figure, which has a defined contrast convertible by the standardization rules. Estimates vary widely by contrast across the literature (categorical high-vs-low ~1.43; Danish registry HR ~2.06 for NLR>=6) -- NLR unit definitions are inconsistent, a major limitation. Predictive only; NLR is a downstream readout of inflammation/stress.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher NLR, incident cardiovascular disease (fully adjusted)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: native: HR reported per 1 SD
cvd.study: Qin 2025, Cardiovascular Diabetology, UK Biobank prospective cohort (45,941 incident CVD events).
cvd.citation: Qin P, Ho FK, Celis-Morales CA, Pell JP. Association between systemic inflammation biomarkers and incident cardiovascular disease in 423,701 individuals: evidence from the UK Biobank cohort. Cardiovasc Diabetol. 2025;24:162. doi:10.1186/s12933-025-02721-9. PMID: 40234895.
cvd.notes: REFINED: replaces the earlier heterogeneous categorical Angkananard pooled OR with a native per-SD HR from a large, fully-adjusted UK Biobank cohort. HR 1.04 (1.04-1.05) per SD; the NLR-CVD relationship was linear. The per-SD effect is small but precisely estimated; categorical high-vs-low contrasts in the older meta-analytic literature look larger (~1.6) because they span a wider exposure range. Predictive, not causal -- Mendelian randomization of genetically-predicted NLR has not supported a causal CAD effect.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: Per one-quartile increase in NLR, overall (any-site) incident cancer
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cancer.study: Cook 2024, Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev, UK Biobank prospective cohort (22,747 incident cancers).
cancer.citation: Cook MB et al. White blood cell count, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, and incident cancer in the UK Biobank. Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev. 2024;33(6):821-829. doi:10.1158/1055-9965.EPI-23-1145.
cancer.notes: REFINED: replaces the earlier non-significant NHANES categorical cancer-MORTALITY estimate with a significant per-quartile HR for cancer INCIDENCE from a large UK Biobank cohort (HR 1.03, 1.02-1.04). Site-specific signals are stronger for kidney, colon, lung and soft-tissue cancers; the overall effect is small. Converted from the quartile metric for comparability (consistent with the NLR all-cause-mortality cell). Predictive; NLR is a downstream readout of inflammation/immunosenescence and possibly preclinical tumor inflammation.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher NLR, incident all-cause dementia
dementia.study: UK Biobank prospective cohort analysis of peripheral immunity and incident dementia (4,239 incident dementia cases).
dementia.citation: UK Biobank prospective cohort: neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio and incident dementia. Median follow-up ~9 years, 361,653 participants. (Peripheral immunity and risk of incident dementia, Mol Psychiatry / Transl Psychiatry 2022-2023 series.)
dementia.notes: REFINED: previously a null cell. Large UK Biobank analysis gives a native per-SD HR 1.16 (1.11-1.20) for incident dementia. A separate biobank analysis reports a weaker Alzheimer's-specific signal (HR ~1.03 per SD), consistent with the cross-marker pattern that inflammation tracks vascular rather than Alzheimer dementia. Status approximate -- exact cohort report not line-verified.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: NLR quartile 4 vs quartile 1, incident frailty over 3 years (multivariable-adjusted odds ratio)
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
frailty.study: Xu 2022, Frontiers in Medicine, Chinese Longitudinal Healthy Longevity Survey, prospective cohort (164 incident frailty cases).
frailty.citation: Xu W, Liang Y, Lin Z. Association between neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio and frailty: the Chinese Longitudinal Healthy Longevity Survey. Front Med (Lausanne). 2022;8:783077. doi:10.3389/fmed.2021.783077. PMID: 35083244.
frailty.notes: REFINED: previously a null cell. Prospective CLHLS analysis of robust/pre-frail elders found NLR Q4 vs Q1 OR 2.06 (1.18-3.59) for incident frailty; dichotomized NLR>=3 gave OR 1.84 (1.14-2.97). Converted from the quartile contrast. Single-cohort, modest size (164 cases), 3-year follow-up -- Tier C. NLR is a downstream stress/inflammation readout, not a causal frailty driver.

modifiability.intervention: No NLR-specific intervention; it falls with resolution of underlying inflammation/illness and improves modestly with exercise and weight loss.
modifiability.effect_size: Not well quantified; NLR normalizes when an acute or chronic inflammatory driver is treated.
modifiability.timeframe: Days to weeks (acute resolution) to months (lifestyle).
modifiability.citation: No dedicated RCT targeting NLR as an endpoint; inferred from CBC normalization in observational data.

profile.mechanism: Hepatically synthesized acute-phase glycoprotein and the terminal substrate of the coagulation cascade. Sits at the intersection of inflammation and thrombosis: elevated levels increase blood viscosity, platelet aggregation, and clot formation, mechanistically linking systemic inflammation to atherothrombotic events.

measurement.method: Clauss clotting assay (functional) or immunoassay (antigen) on a coagulation analyzer.

profile.signal_quality_note: Reasonably standardized assay but, as an acute-phase reactant, levels rise with infection, smoking, pregnancy, and inflammation. Within-person variability is moderate; longer half-life than CRP gives somewhat more stable readings.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline + periodic; available within coagulation panels

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 g/L higher usual fibrinogen, non-vascular mortality (age/sex adjusted)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 0.75 (Plasma fibrinogen population SD ~0.7-0.8 g/L; FSC and general-population cohorts (an SD of ~75 mg/dL is widely used; e.g. used to derive a per-SD RR ~1.31).); k = SD/unit = 0.75

conversion.sd_source: Plasma fibrinogen population SD ~0.7-0.8 g/L; FSC and general-population cohorts (an SD of ~75 mg/dL is widely used; e.g. used to derive a per-SD RR ~1.31).

all_cause_mortality.study: Fibrinogen Studies Collaboration 2005, JAMA, individual-participant meta-analysis of 31 prospective studies.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Fibrinogen Studies Collaboration. Plasma fibrinogen level and the risk of major cardiovascular diseases and nonvascular mortality: an individual participant meta-analysis. JAMA. 2005;294(14):1799-1809. PMID: 16219884.
all_cause_mortality.notes: HR shown is for non-vascular mortality per 1 g/L; total-mortality associations are similarly strong. Age/sex-adjusted estimates attenuate substantially after full risk-factor adjustment. Converted with a population SD of ~0.75 g/L; a per-SD (75 mg/dL) RR of ~1.31 is reported in the literature and is consistent with this conversion.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 g/L higher usual fibrinogen, coronary heart disease (age/sex adjusted)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 1; population SD 0.75 (Plasma fibrinogen population SD ~0.7-0.8 g/L; FSC and general-population cohorts.); k = SD/unit = 0.75

conversion.sd_source: Plasma fibrinogen population SD ~0.7-0.8 g/L; FSC and general-population cohorts.

cvd.study: Fibrinogen Studies Collaboration 2005, JAMA, individual-participant meta-analysis of 31 prospective studies.
cvd.citation: Fibrinogen Studies Collaboration. Plasma fibrinogen level and the risk of major cardiovascular diseases and nonvascular mortality. JAMA. 2005;294(14):1799-1809. PMID: 16219884.
cvd.notes: Age/sex-adjusted HR 2.42 per 1 g/L for CHD; falls to ~1.8 after adjustment for established vascular risk factors. Mendelian randomization studies of fibrinogen-raising variants have NOT consistently supported a causal CHD role -- fibrinogen is largely a marker of the underlying inflammatory/thrombotic milieu rather than an independent causal driver. Field oversells the causal interpretation.

cancer.hr_unit_detail: Highest vs lowest quartile of plasma fibrinogen, overall cancer incidence
cancer.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cancer.study: Luo 2025, Discover Oncology, systematic review and meta-analysis of 12 epidemiological studies (11,834 cancer cases).
cancer.citation: Luo Y, Lu F, Luo L, Li Y. Plasma fibrinogen levels towards cancer incidence: a systematic review and meta-analysis of epidemiological studies. Discov Oncol. 2025;16:844. doi:10.1007/s12672-025-02691-0.
cancer.notes: REFINED: previously a null cell. Meta-analytic HR 1.33 (1.16-1.51) highest vs lowest quartile of plasma fibrinogen for overall cancer incidence; converted from the quartile contrast. The signal is largely driven by SMOKING-RELATED cancers (HR ~1.79) and lung cancer (HR ~1.98) -- residual confounding by smoking is a major caveat, and colorectal cancer was borderline. Fibrinogen is an acute-phase reactant; the association is predictive, not established as causal (no supportive Mendelian randomization). Substantial between-study heterogeneity (I-squared ~67%).

dementia.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher fibrinogen, all-cause dementia (risk-factor adjusted)
dementia.study: van Oijen 2005, Stroke; corroborated by Xu 2020 meta-analysis of fibrinogen and dementia.
dementia.citation: van Oijen M et al. Fibrinogen is associated with an increased risk of Alzheimer disease and vascular dementia. Stroke. 2005;36(12):2637-2641. PMID: 16269641. See also Xu 2020 meta-analysis, PMID: 32081688.
dementia.notes: Per-SD HR 1.30 for all-cause dementia; effect stronger for vascular dementia (HR ~1.76) than Alzheimer's (HR ~1.25), consistent with a vascular mechanism. A 2020 systematic review/meta-analysis supports the association.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: Per 1 SD higher fibrinogen, incident frailty in women over 4 years (fully adjusted odds ratio)
frailty.study: Gale 2013, English Longitudinal Study of Ageing, prospective cohort (women subset; 150 incident frailty cases).
frailty.citation: Gale CR et al. Inflammatory markers and incident frailty in men and women: the English Longitudinal Study of Ageing. Age (Dordr). 2013;35(6):2493-2501. doi:10.1007/s11357-013-9528-9. PMID: 23543263.
frailty.notes: REFINED: previously a null cell. Fully-adjusted OR 1.31 (1.04-1.67) per SD in women -- the only inflammatory marker in ELSA that remained a significant prospective predictor of incident frailty after adjustment. NO association in men (OR 0.89, 0.67-1.18). Sex-specific; Tier C reflects the single-cohort, single-sex evidence.

modifiability.intervention: Smoking cessation, weight loss, and regular physical activity lower fibrinogen; no fibrinogen-specific drug therapy in primary prevention.
modifiability.effect_size: Smoking cessation and exercise reduce fibrinogen modestly (roughly 0.2-0.5 g/L over months); fibrate drugs lower it somewhat but without proven outcome benefit attributable to fibrinogen.
modifiability.citation: Observational and secondary trial data; no RCT demonstrates outcome benefit from fibrinogen lowering per se.

biomarkers.name: Homocysteine

profile.mechanism: Sulfur-containing amino acid intermediate of methionine metabolism. Elevated levels reflect impaired one-carbon metabolism (folate/B12/B6 status, renal function, MTHFR genotype) and have been linked to endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and neurotoxicity. Often grouped with inflammatory/vascular risk markers though it is not a classic acute-phase reactant.

measurement.method: Immunoassay or LC-MS/MS on fasting plasma; sample must be processed promptly as levels rise with delayed serum separation.

profile.signal_quality_note: Analytically reliable but strongly determined by modifiable nutritional status (folate, B12, B6) and renal function; pre-analytic handling matters (levels rise if red cells are not separated quickly). Genetically influenced (MTHFR). Less labile day-to-day than cytokines.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline; recheck after B-vitamin repletion if elevated

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Per 5 micromol/L higher homocysteine, all-cause mortality (dose-response meta-analysis)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 5; population SD 4 (Plasma total homocysteine population SD ~3-5 micromol/L in general adult populations (NHANES / cohort distributions); ~4 micromol/L used as a central estimate.); k = SD/unit = 0.8

conversion.sd_source: Plasma total homocysteine population SD ~3-5 micromol/L in general adult populations (NHANES / cohort distributions); ~4 micromol/L used as a central estimate.

all_cause_mortality.study: Fan 2017, Scientific Reports, dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies (6 studies, 4,110 deaths).
all_cause_mortality.citation: Fan R et al. Association between homocysteine levels and all-cause mortality: a dose-response meta-analysis of prospective studies. Sci Rep. 2017;7:4769. doi:10.1038/s41598-017-05205-3. PMID: 28684797.
all_cause_mortality.notes: REFINED: previously a null/qualitative cell. A linear dose-response meta-analysis gives RR 1.336 (1.254-1.422) per 5 micromol/L (Peng 2015 reported a concordant RR ~1.27). Association is heavily confounded by renal function and frailty. CRITICAL: the decisive evidence is interventional -- B-vitamin trials that effectively lowered homocysteine did NOT reduce mortality. Predictive, not causal.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: Per 5 micromol/L higher homocysteine, incident coronary heart disease (prospective cohorts)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 5; population SD 4 (Plasma total homocysteine population SD ~3-5 micromol/L in general adult populations (NHANES / cohort distributions); ~4 micromol/L used as a central estimate.); k = SD/unit = 0.8

conversion.sd_source: Plasma total homocysteine population SD ~3-5 micromol/L in general adult populations (NHANES / cohort distributions); ~4 micromol/L used as a central estimate.

cvd.study: Humphrey 2008, Mayo Clinic Proceedings, systematic review and meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies.
cvd.citation: Humphrey LL, Fu R, Rogers K, Freeman M, Helfand M. Homocysteine level and coronary heart disease incidence: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Mayo Clin Proc. 2008;83(11):1203-1212. doi:10.4065/83.11.1203. PMID: 18990318.
cvd.notes: REFINED: replaces the earlier categorical interventional RCT estimate with the observational per-5-micromol/L dose-response HR (1.18, 1.10-1.26), making the cell comparable on the per-SD scale and consistent with the homocysteine all-cause-mortality and dementia cells. CRITICAL caveat retained -- this observational association is predictive but almost certainly NOT causal: B-vitamin RCTs (HOPE-2, NORVIT, SEARCH; Cochrane CD006612) that effectively LOWERED homocysteine produced NO reduction in coronary events, MI, or cardiovascular death (pooled RR ~0.98, CI crosses 1; a modest stroke-reduction signal aside). Homocysteine is heavily confounded by renal function and lifestyle. This is where the field most oversells the causal interpretation.

cancer.study: Xu 2018 dose-response meta-analysis (digestive-tract cancers); Mendelian randomization of homocysteine and hormone-related cancers.
cancer.citation: Xu Z et al. Homocysteine and digestive tract cancer risk: a dose-response meta-analysis. J Oncol. 2018;2018:3720684. doi:10.1155/2018/3720684. PMID: 30631354.
cancer.notes: Searched specifically: homocysteine-cancer evidence is genuinely thin and heterogeneous. A dose-response meta-analysis found only a weak digestive-tract-cancer signal (~7% higher risk per 5 micromol/L) and the overall-cancer literature is dominated by case-control studies (subject to reverse causation, since tumors perturb one-carbon metabolism). Mendelian randomization found NO causal effect of homocysteine on hormone-related cancers. No coherent prospective overall-cancer incidence HR with usable CI exists -- left null rather than fabricate a pooled estimate. Honest emptiness.

dementia.hr_unit_detail: Per 5 micromol/L higher homocysteine, Alzheimer-type / incident dementia (dose-response)
dementia.hr_per_sd_basis: per_unit: native HR per 5; population SD 4 (Plasma total homocysteine population SD ~3-5 micromol/L in general adult populations; ~4 micromol/L used as a central estimate.); k = SD/unit = 0.8

conversion.sd_source: Plasma total homocysteine population SD ~3-5 micromol/L in general adult populations; ~4 micromol/L used as a central estimate.

dementia.study: Zhou 2019, dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohorts; consistent with Wald 2011 (8 cohorts, 8,669 participants).
dementia.citation: Zhou F et al. Hyperhomocysteinemia and risk of incident cognitive outcomes: an updated dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies. Ageing Res Rev. 2019;51:55-66. PMID: 30826501.
dementia.notes: Robust observational dose-response association (~9-15% higher dementia/AD risk per 5 micromol/L). However, B-vitamin trials targeting cognition show mixed results -- some slowing of brain atrophy in high-homocysteine subgroups (VITACOG) but no consistent prevention of clinical dementia. Predictive association is genuine; causal benefit of lowering is unproven.

frailty.hr_unit_detail: High total homocysteine (>=15 micromol/L) vs lower, incident frailty in older men over ~5 years
frailty.hr_per_sd_basis: categorical exposure — not placed on the per-SD scale
frailty.study: Wong 2013, J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci, Health in Men Study, prospective cohort of community-dwelling men aged 70-88.
frailty.citation: Wong YYE, Almeida OP, McCaul KA, et al. Homocysteine, frailty, and all-cause mortality in older men: the Health in Men Study. J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. 2013;68(5):590-598. doi:10.1093/gerona/gls211. PMID: 23070880.
frailty.notes: REFINED: previously a null cell. Prospective Health in Men Study found high tHcy (>=15 micromol/L) associated with incident frailty at HR 1.25 -- but the CI crosses 1 (0.95-1.65), so the longitudinal signal is borderline and not statistically significant, despite a clear cross-sectional association (prevalent-frailty OR 1.49). Reported against a fixed clinical threshold rather than a quantile, so flagged categorical (not converted). Homocysteine-frailty associations are heavily confounded by renal function and B-vitamin status; predictive at best, with no causal support.

modifiability.intervention: Folic acid plus vitamin B12 (and B6) supplementation reliably lowers plasma homocysteine.
modifiability.effect_size: B-vitamin supplementation lowers homocysteine by roughly 25-30%; however this lowering did NOT translate into reduced cardiovascular events or mortality in large RCTs.
modifiability.citation: Marti-Carvajal AJ et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017;8:CD006612. JAMA Intern Med 2010 meta-analysis of 8 RCTs.

biomarkers.comparability_note: Homocysteine standardizes normally for mortality, CVD and dementia; only the frailty cell is off-scale, because that study reported a clinical >=15 micromol/L cutpoint contrast rather than a continuous estimate.

profile.mechanism: Primary intracellular iron-storage protein; circulating ferritin reflects body iron stores but is ALSO a positive acute-phase reactant that rises with inflammation independent of iron. This dual identity makes elevated ferritin a mixed signal of iron overload, hepatocellular injury, and/or systemic inflammation.

measurement.method: Immunoassay (chemiluminescent or immunoturbidimetric) on a standard analyzer.

profile.signal_quality_note: Interpretation is genuinely ambiguous: an elevated value may indicate iron overload, an acute-phase response, liver disease, malignancy, or metabolic syndrome. Without concurrent CRP, transferrin saturation, and clinical context, a single ferritin is difficult to interpret as an inflammation marker specifically.
profile.testing_cadence: baseline; interpret alongside CRP and transferrin saturation

all_cause_mortality.hr_unit_detail: Highest vs lowest ferritin quartile, all-cause mortality (overall population)
all_cause_mortality.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
all_cause_mortality.study: NHANES III / NHANES 1999-2010 pooled analysis of iron biomarkers and mortality.
all_cause_mortality.citation: Iron metabolic biomarkers and the mortality risk in the general population: a nationwide population-based cohort study. J Endocr Soc. 2024;8(6):bvae063. PMC11017327.
all_cause_mortality.notes: Adjusted HRs for Q2/Q3/Q4 vs Q1 were 1.07/1.05/1.13; exact CI for Q4 not captured here so CI fields left null and status set to approximate. The ferritin-mortality relationship is often U- or J-shaped (both iron deficiency and overload carry risk), and the elevated-end signal attenuates after adjusting for inflammation, underscoring confounding -- the quartile conversion assumes monotonicity that may not strictly hold.

cvd.hr_unit_detail: Highest vs lowest serum ferritin quartile, cardiovascular mortality (general population, fully adjusted)
cvd.hr_per_sd_basis: quartile contrast; k = 1/2.54 (normal-approx extreme-group SD separation)
cvd.study: Sun 2024, J Endocr Soc, NHANES pooled nationwide cohort of iron biomarkers and mortality.
cvd.citation: Sun Y, Peng W, Lin S, Cui J, Lu J. Iron metabolic biomarkers and the mortality risk in the general population: a nationwide population-based cohort study. J Endocr Soc. 2024;8(6):bvae063. doi:10.1210/jendso/bvae063. PMC11017327.
cvd.notes: REFINED: previously a qualitative null cell. The NHANES analysis gives a fully-adjusted Q4-vs-Q1 HR 1.13 (1.01-1.28) for cardiovascular mortality -- barely significant. Converted from the quartile contrast (same cohort and metric as the ferritin all-cause-mortality cell). The ferritin-CVD relationship is often U/J-shaped and the elevated-end signal attenuates heavily after adjusting for inflammation, so the quartile conversion assumes a monotonicity that may not strictly hold; status approximate. Mendelian randomization of iron status gives inconsistent CVD signals -- predictive/confounded, not established causal.

cancer.study: Prospective cohort and meta-analytic evidence on prediagnostic ferritin and cancer incidence is heterogeneous and direction-inconsistent.
